all_picks.headline: Elite hitter vs HR-prone pitcher at a neutral park — Henderson should do damage.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Gunnar Henderson is one of the best young hitters in baseball. He's facing Zack Littell, who has a brutal 2.52 HR/9 this season — that's among the worst in the league. Littell also has a weak 14.0 K%, meaning he doesn't miss bats and relies on contact management. Against an elite hitter like Henderson, that's a recipe for extra-base hits.

Camden Yards is neutral for runs (1.01 PF) but slightly HR-suppressing (0.93 PF). The weather shows 80°F with 5 mph wind blowing in from right field — not a significant factor. Henderson bats from the left side, which means he's pulling balls toward the short porch in right field.

The market has Henderson TB Over 1.5 at -104 on DraftKings. Given Littell's HR/9 and Henderson's elite bat, I'm projecting Henderson to clear 1.5 TB in approximately 57% of outcomes. That's enough edge to play.
all_picks.the_market: Henderson TB Over 1.5 at -104 on DraftKings, -110 on BetMGM. Line is fair but we have edge given Littell's profile.
all_picks.weather_park: Camden Yards, 80°F, 5 mph wind in from RF — slight HR suppression but not significant.
all_picks.case_against: Henderson could go 0-for-4 with hard contact that finds gloves. Littell could pitch around him if the game situation dictates. The 0.93 HR PF means some fly balls that would be HR elsewhere stay in the park.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Henderson getting 4+ ABs, making hard contact against a pitcher who doesn't miss bats, and collecting at least one XBH or two singles.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Littell gives up bombs. Henderson hits bombs. Do the math.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Sunday MLB-only slate with 15 games. Several data confidence issues flagged, but Drew Rasmussen vs Arizona at Tropicana Field stands out as the cleanest edge on the board — elite SP in a pitcher-friendly dome against a struggling offense.
all_picks.executive_summary: Fifteen-game MLB Sunday slate with no NBA or NHL action. The cleanest play jumps off the page immediately: Drew Rasmussen at home in the Trop against Arizona's Merrill Kelly. Rasmussen has been dominant in June — 1.00 ERA, 34:3 K:BB over his last four starts — and Tropicana Field's pitcher-friendly environment plus Kelly's 2.52 HR/9 creates a clear under angle. The Cleveland-Seattle matchup offers another quality pitching duel at a depressed 7.5 total. Hunter Brown's return gives Houston a live arm against Detroit in a weather-suppressed Comerica Park spot. The ladder rides with the Rays Under 7.5 — Rasmussen's floor is as high as anyone's on this board.
all_picks.slate_analysis:
Sunday's board features 15 MLB games with no NBA or NHL in play. The pricing climate is relatively soft — several totals sit at 9.0 or above, and the moneyline favorites are mostly in the -130 to -170 range. Data confidence is a concern on multiple games: CIN-PIT, BAL-WSH, TOR-TEX, TB-ARI, MIL-CHC, and SF-ATL all show confidence below 0.5, which limits the candidate pool. The ATL-SF game shows Chris Sale starting, but web search confirms Sale has been on the 60-day IL since late June 2025 with a fractured rib — that game's data is unreliable and I'm passing entirely.

The top candidates that earned a hard look: (1) TB-ARI Under 7.5 — Rasmussen has been elite in June with a 1.00 ERA and 0.59 WHIP over 27 innings, facing Kelly who has a 5.71 ERA and 2.52 HR/9 but in a pitcher-friendly dome that suppresses HR. (2) CLE-SEA Under 7.5 — Gavin Williams (3.82 ERA, 28.5 K%) vs Emerson Hancock (3.60 ERA, 1.02 WHIP) is a quality pitching matchup at a low total. (3) DET-HOU Under 8.0 — Hunter Brown's return (1.40 ERA in limited 2026 action) against Flaherty at Comerica with wind blowing in 9 mph suppressing HR by ~12%. (4) BOS-NYY Under 8.0 — Sonny Gray (2.95 ERA) vs Carlos Rodón at Fenway, two quality arms in a rivalry game. (5) MIN-COL Under 9.0 — 15 mph wind blowing in at Target Field suppresses HR by ~18%, both pitchers mediocre but weather is a significant factor. (6) Gunnar Henderson TB Over 1.5 — facing Zack Littell (2.52 HR/9, 14.0 K%) at Camden Yards.

What got cut: The ATL-SF game was eliminated entirely due to the Sale data error — can't trust any of that game's intel. BAL-WSH moneyline looked appealing (Bradish vs Littell) but -196 juice exceeds our -150 cap for straight bets. MIL-CHC had Woodruff returning but only 36 IP this season and data confidence at 0.4 — sample too thin. STL-MIA looked like a coin flip with no clear edge. LAA-ATH had two mediocre pitchers and no directional signal. SD-LAD had King vs Sheehan but Dodgers offense is too potent to trust an under, and Padres ML at +120 doesn't clear my edge threshold against LA's lineup.

The final six picks: TB Under 7.5 (Rasmussen dominance + Trop + weak ARI offense), CLE-SEA Under 7.5 (two quality arms, low total, wind in), DET-HOU Under 8.0 (Brown's return + weather suppression), BOS-NYY Under 8.0 (Gray vs Rodón, two quality arms), MIN-COL Under 9.0 (extreme wind suppression), and Gunnar Henderson TB Over 1.5 (elite hitter vs HR-prone pitcher). The ladder pick is TB Under 7.5 — Rasmussen's floor is the highest on the board, the Trop suppresses offense, and Kelly has been hittable but the total is already low enough that we're not asking for a shutout.

autopsy.post_mortem: Nationals beat Baltimore 6-4 on June 28. Zack Littell was actually effective — he allowed only 2 runs on 3 hits over 5 innings, not the meatball outing the thesis projected. More critically, the pick ignored a glaring 2026 Henderson red flag: he ranked 329th of 343 qualified hitters in two-strike batting average (.074), was on pace for a career-high strikeout rate (31.2%), and was slashing just .214/.269/.423 on the season. The 'elite hitter vs. HR-prone pitcher' frame was built on Henderson's career reputation, not his 2026 form — and Littell's prior outing against Baltimore (5 scoreless innings in May) was available data that was not incorporated.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Before targeting a player prop on an 'elite hitter,' validate current-season K% and two-strike performance — if a hitter's strikeout rate has spiked more than 5 percentage points above career norms, discount the TB Over projection by at least one tier regardless of opposing pitcher profile. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule.)
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one loss does not confirm the pattern, but the underlying data mismatch (career reputation vs. 2026 regression) is worth flagging as a model blind spot to monitor.

all_picks.headline: 15 mph wind blowing in at Target Field suppresses HR by 18% — that's a massive weather edge.
all_picks.the_thesis:
This isn't about the pitchers — Connor Prielipp (5.17 ERA) and Ryan Feltner (4.79 ERA) are both mediocre. This is about the weather. Target Field is showing 15 mph wind blowing in from right field, which suppresses HR by approximately 18%. That's one of the most significant weather factors on the board today.

Both offenses are average — Minnesota at 4.87 R/G and Colorado at 4.64 R/G. But Colorado's offense is built on power (they play half their games at Coors), and when you take away the long ball, they become significantly less dangerous. The Rockies have historically struggled on the road, and this weather profile makes it even harder.

The market has this at 9.0 with the under at -110 on BetMGM. Given the extreme wind suppression, my fair line is closer to 8.0-8.5. That's enough edge to play, even with two mediocre starters.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 9.0 and has held. Under getting slight juice at -110 to -115. No significant movement.
all_picks.weather_park: Target Field, 76°F, 15 mph wind blowing in from RF — suppresses HR by ~18%. This is the play.
all_picks.case_against: Both pitchers walk too many batters (Prielipp 9.3 BB%, Feltner 10.1 BB%). If they can't throw strikes, the walks will pile up and runs will score even without HR. Minnesota's bullpen has been inconsistent.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: The wind keeping balls in the park, Colorado's power being neutralized, and both teams settling for singles and doubles instead of extra-base hits.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: 15 mph wind blowing in? The Rockies' power hitters just became slap hitters.

all_picks.headline: Sonny Gray vs Carlos Rodón at Fenway — two quality arms in a rivalry game that should stay tight.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Sonny Gray has been excellent this season — 2.95 ERA, 1.19 WHIP, and a solid 21.1 K% with just 6.4 BB%. He's been consistent and durable, averaging 25+ IP per three-start stretch. Against a Yankees lineup that's potent (4.94 R/G), Gray will need to be sharp, but he's shown he can handle big moments.

Carlos Rodón has been limited this season (41.1 IP) but effective when healthy — 3.70 ERA with an elite 26.6 K%. His 12.7 BB% is concerning, but against a Boston offense averaging just 3.99 R/G, he should be able to work around the walks. Rodón's 0.66 HR/9 is elite — he doesn't give up the long ball.

Fenway Park is hitter-friendly (1.05 runs PF), which is the main concern here. But with two quality arms and a total at 8.0, the market is already accounting for the park. My fair line is 7.5-8.0, so we're right at the edge — but the pitching matchup gives us enough to play the under.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 8.0 and has held across all books. Under getting slight juice at -105 to -108. Balanced action.
all_picks.weather_park: Fenway Park — hitter-friendly (1.05 runs PF) but HR-suppressing (0.96). Weather not a factor for night game.
all_picks.case_against: Fenway is a bandbox, and the Yankees lineup can explode at any moment. Rodón's 12.7 BB% means free baserunners, and if Judge or Soto get hot, this could sail over quickly. Gray has a 1.06 HR/9 — one mistake to the wrong hitter changes everything.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6+ innings, combined 5 or fewer runs through 7 innings, and the bullpens holding serve late.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Rivalry game, two aces, Fenway under the lights. Sometimes the best bet is the boring one.

autopsy.post_mortem: The under was alive and thriving through nine innings — Sonny Gray was dominant, taking a no-hit bid into the 8th inning (7.1 IP, 1 H, 9 K), and Rodón went 5 IP with the game sitting at a combined 4 runs through regulation. The under died in extras: Chapman blew a save in the 9th when Abreu's throwing error allowed the tying run, the Yankees then scored twice in the 10th on another Abreu error, and Boston rallied for three in the bottom of the 10th — capped by Jarren Duran's walk-off single — to push the final to 5-4 in 10 innings. Through nine innings this was a clean under; the 9th-inning blown save and a two-error extra-inning sequence by a two-time Gold Glover are the specific culprits.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a textbook extras/blown-save variance event, not a systemic modeling failure.

all_picks.headline: Hunter Brown's return, wind blowing in at Comerica, and two offenses that don't scare anyone.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Hunter Brown is back and looking sharp. After missing over two months with a Grade 2 shoulder strain, he returned on June 16 and allowed just one run on four hits with seven strikeouts over 5.2 innings against Toronto. His 2026 numbers are limited (19.1 IP) but elite — 1.40 ERA with 28 strikeouts. The Astros' ace is healthy and dealing.

Jack Flaherty has been inconsistent (5.35 ERA, 1.57 WHIP), but he's facing a Houston offense that's been decimated by injuries. The Astros are averaging 4.47 R/G but have been without Carlos Correa and Jose Altuve for stretches. Detroit's offense (4.08 R/G) isn't much better.

The weather is the kicker here. Comerica Park already plays neutral-to-pitcher-friendly (0.96 runs PF), but today's 9 mph wind blowing in from left field suppresses HR by approximately 12%. That's a significant factor in a game with two fly-ball pitchers. The market has this at 8.0 on DraftKings with the under at -105 — that's value given the weather and Brown's return.
all_picks.the_market: Total varies by book — 8.0 on DK/MGM, 8.5 on FanDuel. DraftKings has the best number at 8.0 with -105 juice. Line has moved down from opening.
all_picks.weather_park: Comerica Park, 74°F, 9 mph wind blowing in from LF — suppresses HR by ~12%. Significant factor.
all_picks.case_against: Brown is on a pitch count after his injury — if he exits after 4-5 innings, Houston's bullpen has been shaky. Flaherty's 11.1 BB% means free baserunners, and if Houston strings together walks with hits, this could sail over.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Brown going 5+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, the wind keeping balls in the park, and two average offenses staying quiet.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Wind blowing in, Brown blowing fastballs by hitters. Math checks out.

autopsy.post_mortem: The pick modeled Flaherty as a healthy (if shaky) starter, but he was actually returning from the IL with a left peroneal strain and had made only one rehab start at Double-A Erie — that context was either missed or understated. Brown was also a pitch-count liability: his prior start lasted just 3 innings on 85 pitches, a red flag the thesis glossed over. Flaherty imploded for 5 ER in 4 IP, and Brown's early exit handed the game to bullpens that couldn't hold the line — the 12-run final (HOU 7, DET 5) was baked in the moment both starters were effectively operating as 4-inning arms coming off injury.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When either starter is returning from the IL with fewer than 2 healthy starts logged, apply a minimum +0.75 run adjustment to the projected total per IL-returning arm — and flag the game for total-side caution regardless of ERA optics; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the pattern of IL-return starts inflating totals is worth tracking across the next 10 comparable matchups before codifying.

all_picks.headline: Two quality arms, two weak offenses, and a total that's already low — but not low enough.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Gavin Williams vs Emerson Hancock is a sneaky good pitching matchup. Williams has a 3.82 ERA with a 1.14 WHIP and an excellent 28.5 K% — he misses bats and limits damage. Hancock has been even better by WHIP (1.02) with a 3.60 ERA and solid 24.2 K%. Neither pitcher is a household name, but both are pitching well above replacement level.

The offenses here are anemic. Cleveland is averaging just 3.92 R/G — that's bottom-five in baseball. Seattle is barely better at 4.01 R/G. When you combine two below-average offenses with two above-average starters, the math points to a low-scoring game. Progressive Field is neutral (0.97 runs PF), and the weather shows 81°F with 7 mph wind blowing in from right field — not a significant factor but slightly suppressive.

The market has this at 7.5 with the under juiced to -114. My fair line is closer to 6.5-7.0 given the pitching matchup and offensive profiles. That's enough edge to play.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and has held. Under is getting moderate juice at -114 to -118, suggesting some sharp lean toward the under.
all_picks.weather_park: Progressive Field, 81°F, 7 mph wind in from RF — slight HR suppression, net neutral.
all_picks.case_against: Both pitchers have HR/9 rates above 1.2 (Williams 1.40, Hancock 1.27), meaning one mistake pitch could flip the script. If either starter exits early due to pitch count or inefficiency, the bullpens could give up runs.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6+ innings with combined 4 or fewer runs, bullpens holding serve, and two weak offenses staying true to form.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two offenses that couldn't score at a Little League game. We'll take the under.

The starters did their jobs; this was a late-inning bullpen collapse compounded by a roster move nobody could have priced in.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean variance loss driven by a specific 8th-inning bullpen meltdown and a same-day IL activation; no systemic flaw identified.

all_picks.headline: Rasmussen has been untouchable in June, and the Trop is where offense goes to die.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Drew Rasmussen is pitching at an elite level right now. Over his last four starts in June, he's posted a 1.00 ERA with a 0.59 WHIP and a ridiculous 34:3 K:BB ratio across 27 innings. That's not a hot streak — that's dominance. His season line sits at 2.62 ERA with a 0.88 WHIP over 86 innings, and he's logged nine quality starts in 15 appearances. The 2025 All-Star has found another gear.

Merrill Kelly is the opposite story. His 5.71 ERA and 1.52 WHIP tell you he's been hittable, but the more concerning number is his 2.52 HR/9 — that's among the worst in baseball. The good news for Kelly is he's pitching in Tropicana Field, which has a 0.92 HR park factor and suppresses runs (0.95 PF). The dome neutralizes some of his fly-ball issues, but it also means Arizona's offense — already averaging just 4.26 R/G — will struggle to generate crooked numbers against Rasmussen's elite stuff.

The market has this total at 7.5 with the under at -102 on FanDuel. My fair line is closer to 6.5-7.0 given Rasmussen's recent form and the park factors. That's a significant edge. The Rays bullpen has been solid, and even if Kelly exits early, Arizona's pen will be pitching in the same run-suppressing environment.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and hasn't moved. The under is getting slight juice at -102 to -105 across books, suggesting balanced action. No sharp movement detected.
all_picks.weather_park: Indoor game at Tropicana Field — no weather factors. Park suppresses both runs (0.95) and HR (0.92).
all_picks.case_against: Kelly's struggles could lead to an early exit, putting the game in the hands of bullpens. If Arizona's pen implodes or the Rays tack on late runs against a tired Kelly, this could sail over. Rasmussen's pitch count management has been a factor this season — if he exits after 5-6 innings, the Rays pen needs to hold.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Rasmussen going 6+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, Kelly surviving long enough to keep the game close, and the Trop doing what it does — suppressing offense.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: The Trop: where fly balls go to die and unders go to cash.
all_picks.ladder_note: Highest floor on the board. Rasmussen has been elite, the park suppresses runs, and we're getting plus-money on a total that should be lower. This is the ladder lock.

all_picks.headline: Buxton's power against Lorenzen's 7.11 ERA and 1.54 HR/9.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Michael Lorenzen has been one of the worst starters in baseball this season with a 7.11 ERA, 1.83 WHIP, and 1.54 HR/9. He's giving up the long ball at an alarming rate, and Byron Buxton is one of the most dangerous power hitters in the American League when healthy.

Buxton's total bases line is set at 1.5, with the over at -130 at BetMGM. That's a bit juicy, but the matchup is elite. Lorenzen's 1.54 HR/9 means he's giving up a home run roughly every 6 innings — Buxton only needs one extra-base hit to cash this.

Target Field has a 0.97 HR factor, which is neutral, but Lorenzen's struggles transcend park factors. He's been hittable everywhere.
all_picks.the_market: BetMGM has the over at -130, DraftKings at -101. The DraftKings line is better value, but BetMGM is the best book for the boys. The market hasn't moved despite Lorenzen's struggles.
all_picks.weather_park: Target Field is a neutral park (0.97 HR factor). No significant weather concerns.
all_picks.case_against: Buxton is injury-prone and may not get a full complement of at-bats. If Lorenzen gets knocked out early, Buxton may face the Rockies' bullpen instead. The -130 juice is steep for a prop.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Buxton getting 3-4 at-bats against Lorenzen and connecting on at least one extra-base hit. One double or home run cashes this.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Lorenzen's 1.54 HR/9 meets Buxton's bat. This is a math problem, not a gamble.

Saturday's board features 16 MLB games across both leagues, no NBA or NHL action to supplement. The Twins-Rockies game features two shaky starters (Paredes with 12.8% K rate, Lorenzen with 7.11 ERA) and a 9.5 total that's already priced in the chaos.

The final six picks survived because they combine elite pitching, favorable park factors, and market inefficiency. The Dodgers-Padres Under gets Yamamoto at Petco with a 0.93 runs park factor. The Giants-Braves Under pairs Webb's return with Oracle Park's 0.85 HR factor. The Brewers ML backs Harrison's 30% K rate against Peterson's bloated 6.09 ERA. The Tigers-Astros Under stacks Valdez's ground-ball profile with wind blowing in at Comerica. The Buxton TB Over exploits Lorenzen's 1.54 HR/9 and 7.11 ERA. The White Sox ML at -122 backs Davis Martin's 3.18 ERA against Wacha in a near-even-money spot. The ladder pick is the Giants-Braves Under — Webb and Elder in the most pitcher-friendly park in baseball, both offenses middling, and the market hasn't moved off 7.5.

autopsy.post_mortem: Lorenzen actually held up his end of the bargain — he mixed speeds and kept the Twins off solid contact all night, per Twins Daily: 'he effectively mixed speeds and kept the Minnesota bats away from solid contact.' Buxton's only plate appearance against Lorenzen in the first inning ended with him getting hit by a pitch (HBP), earning a base but zero total bases. In his later at-bat against the Rockies bullpen, he popped up a hanging sweeper to end a rally. Colorado won 8-5 behind Hunter Goodman's 3-homer, 5-RBI night — the Twins lost the game, but the prop died specifically because Lorenzen, despite his 7.11 ERA, was sharp on this particular night and Buxton's two plate appearances produced an HBP and a popup. The matchup thesis was sound; Lorenzen simply had one of his better outings on the wrong day.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. A pitcher with a 7.11 ERA will occasionally have a good start — this is the definition of variance, not a model flaw.

all_picks.headline: Davis Martin's 3.18 ERA against Michael Wacha in a near-even-money spot.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Davis Martin has been one of the most underrated pitchers in baseball this season with a 3.18 ERA, 1.20 WHIP, and 0.62 HR/9. He's been dominant at home, and the White Sox's 4.83 R/G gives him run support. Michael Wacha on the other side has been solid but not spectacular — 3.48 ERA, 1.16 WHIP, and 0.89 HR/9.

The market has Chicago at -122, which implies about 55% win probability. I'm building a fair line around 57%, which gives us 2 cents of edge. Not huge, but this is a near-even-money spot where the home team has the better pitcher.

Guaranteed Rate Field has a 1.08 HR factor, which slightly favors the over, but Martin's 0.62 HR/9 suggests he can suppress the long ball even in a hitter-friendly environment.
all_picks.the_market: Chicago opened at -122 and hasn't moved. The public is split, but the home team with the better pitcher at near-even-money is the sharp side.
all_picks.weather_park: Guaranteed Rate Field is a slight hitter's park (1.08 HR factor), but Martin's HR suppression should neutralize that.
all_picks.case_against: The Royals have been playing better lately, and Wacha is a solid veteran who can keep games close. If Martin doesn't have his best stuff, the Royals could steal one on the road.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Martin going 6+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, the White Sox's offense providing run support, and Chicago's bullpen closing it out.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Davis Martin at home against the Royals. Sometimes the boring play is the right play.

all_picks.headline: Wind blowing in at Comerica with Framber Valdez on the mound.
all_picks.the_thesis:
The IntelPack flags a weather signal: wind 9 mph blowing in from left field, suppressing HR by approximately 11%. Comerica Park already has a 0.95 HR factor — add the wind and this becomes one of the most pitcher-friendly environments on the board today.

Framber Valdez is a ground-ball machine with a 3.91 ERA and 0.91 HR/9. His sinker plays perfectly in this environment. Kai-Wei Teng on the other side has a 4.03 ERA and 1.35 HR/9, but the wind will suppress his biggest vulnerability. Both offenses are middling — Detroit at 4.06 R/G, Houston at 4.43 R/G.

The market has this at 8.5 with the under at -105 at BetMGM. I'm building a fair line around 7.5-8.0, which gives us 8-10 cents of edge on the under.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 8.5 and hasn't moved. BetMGM has the best under price at -105. The public is split, but the wind signal suggests the under is the sharp side.
all_picks.weather_park: Comerica Park with 9 mph wind blowing in from left field, suppressing HR by ~11%. Temperature 69°F. This is a significant weather edge.
all_picks.case_against: Valdez's 8.7% BB rate is elevated — if he walks the ballpark, the Astros could score without needing the long ball. Teng's 10.5% BB rate is even worse. If both pitchers are wild, this could get sloppy.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Valdez going 6+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, the wind suppressing any late-game power, and both offenses struggling to manufacture runs without the long ball.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Wind blowing in at Comerica. Valdez's sinker is about to have a field day.

autopsy.post_mortem: The wind-suppression thesis was built around preventing home runs, but the game blew up on contact hits, not power. Teng loaded the bases in the 3rd via a walk, an Altuve error, and an HBP, then hung a curveball that Kerry Carpenter crushed for a grand slam to put Detroit up 4-3. Valdez's own line was a disaster — 6 IP, 8 H, 4 ER — as the Astros peppered him with singles and doubles rather than homers, and the Detroit bullpen then surrendered the lead late, finishing 8-6. The model correctly identified HR suppression but failed to account for the fact that both pitchers' elevated walk rates (flagged in the 'case against') would create traffic that contact-heavy offenses could exploit without needing the long ball at all.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When both starters carry BB rates above 8.5%, discount wind-based HR suppression signals by at least 50% on totals, because base-on-balls traffic enables run-scoring without home runs — but N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The walk-rate vulnerability was explicitly noted in the 'case against' and was the actual mechanism of failure; watching for pattern before codifying.

all_picks.headline: Webb returns to Oracle Park — the most HR-suppressing venue in baseball.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Logan Webb is back from his knee injury and has looked sharp in his return, posting a 3.35 ERA and 0.54 HR/9 this season. Oracle Park's 0.85 HR park factor is the lowest in baseball, and Webb's sinker-heavy approach plays perfectly here. Bryce Elder on the other side has been solid with a 3.71 ERA, and the Braves' 4.85 R/G won't translate at Oracle.

The market has this at 7.5 at BetMGM and 8.0 at FanDuel/DraftKings — that's a half-run of value at BetMGM. Webb's 5.9% BB rate keeps traffic off the bases, and Elder's 7.3% BB rate is manageable. Neither pitcher is going to walk the ballpark.

This is the ladder pick because it has the highest floor on the board. Two competent starters, the most pitcher-friendly park in baseball, and near-even-money juice at BetMGM. The Braves and Giants are both middling offenses — this is a 3-2 or 4-3 game.
all_picks.the_market: BetMGM has this at 7.5 (-102 under) while FanDuel and DraftKings have it at 8.0. That's a half-run of value at BetMGM — the boys should be betting there.
all_picks.weather_park: Oracle Park is the most HR-suppressing venue in baseball with a 0.85 HR factor. Night game, typical San Francisco marine layer expected.
all_picks.case_against: Webb is coming off a knee injury and may not have his full velocity. The Braves have power bats who can go deep even at Oracle. If Webb's sinker isn't biting, the Braves could tag him early.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Webb going 6+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, Elder keeping the Giants' middling offense in check, and Oracle Park suppressing any late-game power.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Oracle Park at night is where offense goes to die. Webb's sinker is the undertaker.
all_picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board — two competent starters, the most pitcher-friendly park in baseball, and near-even-money juice. This is the ladder pick.

all_picks.headline: Yamamoto at Petco is the cleanest pitching spot on the board.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Yoshinobu Yamamoto has been the best pitcher in baseball over his last two starts, retiring 45 of 46 batters faced across those outings. His 2.65 ERA and 0.87 WHIP this season are elite, and he's facing a Padres lineup that ranks dead last in wOBA against right-handed pitching. Petco Park's 0.93 runs park factor and 0.91 HR factor make this the most pitcher-friendly environment on the board.

Randy Vásquez on the other side isn't an ace, but his 4.17 ERA is serviceable, and the Dodgers' 5.17 R/G won't translate fully at Petco. The market has this at 8.0 across all three books with no movement — the public hasn't pushed it despite Yamamoto's dominance. I'm building a fair line around 7.5, which gives us roughly 9 cents of edge on the under at -110.

The Dodgers are the best offense in baseball, but Petco suppresses their power, and Yamamoto's splitter has been unhittable. The Padres simply can't score against elite right-handed pitching. This is a 3-2 or 4-2 game.

The Reds ML against Pittsburgh looked interesting with Chase Burns on the mound, but the Pirates' 5.04 R/G and Burns' 1.06 HR/9 create variance I don't love at -118. autopsy.post_mortem: The under was never really alive. The thesis correctly identified Yamamoto's dominance — he was efficient through 6 innings, facing only 24 batters and allowing just 1 run (a Gavin Sheets solo HR in the 5th). But the pick was built on a false premise about the other half of the equation: Vásquez's ERA of 4.17 masked a catastrophic recent split — a 6.91 ERA over his last five starts with a 1.94 WHIP and batters hitting .345 against him. The model used his season-long ERA as a 'serviceable' anchor when the rolling data screamed implosion risk. That implosion arrived in the 6th: Vásquez recorded only one out before being pulled, a Will Wagner fielding error opened the floodgates, and Mookie Betts capped a 9-run inning with a 3-run homer. The Dodgers added 4 more in the 8th (Tucker and Rushing each homered) to reach 15. The under was dead by the middle of the 6th inning at 10+ runs. There was also a DATA wrinkle: the Padres announced a Kyle Hart opener the day of the game, meaning Vásquez faced the Dodgers lineup cold in innings 2–5 rather than from the jump — but Hart's 2-inning stint actually held the score to 1-0, so the opener didn't cause the blowup. The core failure was treating Vásquez's season ERA as representative when his last-month ERA was nearly 7.00.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When a starting pitcher's rolling 5-start ERA exceeds 6.50 and his WHIP exceeds 1.90, exclude him from under/low-total constructions regardless of season-long ERA — the regression to mean has already arrived; N=1 here, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one Vásquez implosion does not confirm the pattern, but the split between his season ERA (4.17) and rolling ERA (6.91) was a red flag that was available pre-game and was not weighted in the model.

all_picks.headline: Buxton's power against a soft-tossing righty with a 1.59 HR/9—this is a smash spot.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Byron Buxton is one of the most dangerous power hitters in baseball when healthy, and he's facing a perfect matchup tonight. Tomoyuki Sugano has a 4.31 ERA, 13.7% K rate, and 1.59 HR/9—he doesn't miss bats and gives up hard contact. Buxton thrives against pitchers who can't put him away.

Target Field is neutral (0.98 runs PF, 0.97 HR PF), so there's no park factor working against us. Buxton has been hitting the ball hard all season, and he's due for a multi-base game. The over 1.5 total bases at -135 is the play.

I have Buxton at 60% to go over 1.5 total bases, which implies fair odds of -150. We're getting 15 cents of value on this prop.
all_picks.the_market: Buxton's total bases line is 1.5 with the over at -135 (BetMGM). DraftKings has it at +102, which is a significant discrepancy—we're taking the BetMGM price because it's still value.
all_picks.weather_park: Target Field is outdoor—74°F, light wind. Park factor is neutral.
all_picks.case_against: Buxton is injury-prone and could be pulled early if the game gets out of hand. Sugano has been better at limiting extra-base hits than his HR/9 suggests. Buxton could also go 0-for-4 with 3 strikeouts—he's volatile.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Buxton getting at least one extra-base hit (double, triple, or HR) against a pitcher who gives up hard contact. A single and a double would also cash this.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Sugano's K rate is lower than my fantasy team's batting average.

all_picks.headline: Petco Park is where offense goes to die—even with two shaky starters, the park will bail us out.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Petco Park is one of the most pitcher-friendly parks in baseball with a 0.93 runs PF and 0.91 HR PF. It suppresses offense across the board, and even mediocre pitchers look good there. Walker Buehler (3.96 ERA, 0.62 HR/9) has been solid, and Roki Sasaki (4.76 ERA, 1.72 HR/9) has struggled but should benefit from the park.

The Padres offense has been weak (3.91 R/G), and while the Dodgers are better (5.22 R/G), they're facing a pitcher who's been better at home. The under 7.5 at -105 is the play. I have this game at 6.8 runs, which gives us about 8 cents of edge on the under.

The park is the edge here. Even if both starters struggle, the bullpens should keep this game low-scoring.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and has held. No movement, which suggests the market is comfortable with this number.
all_picks.weather_park: Petco Park is outdoor—68°F, light wind. Park factor is the story: 0.93 runs, 0.91 HR.
all_picks.case_against: Sasaki's 1.72 HR/9 is concerning, and the Dodgers have some dangerous hitters who could take him deep. If Buehler doesn't go deep into the game, the Padres bullpen could be tested.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Petco Park suppressing offense, both starters going 5+ innings, and a low-scoring 4-3 or 3-2 type game.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Petco Park: where fly balls go to die in the marine layer.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis leaned on Petco Park suppressing offense, but the park was never the problem — the model missed a critical asymmetry in the pitching matchup. Sasaki walked five batters in four innings, surrendering a Ty France three-run homer in the 2nd after walking the first two hitters he faced, putting the Padres up 3-1 before the game was two innings old. The under was effectively dead by the 8th, when Dodgers reliever Jonathan Hernández allowed four runs on a single, two walks, another single, a double, and a final single to make it 7-1 — a total that had nothing to do with park factors and everything to do with Sasaki's well-documented road command issues (6.19 ERA on the road in 2026) being ignored in the model.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When a starter has a road ERA 2+ runs higher than his home ERA AND a WHIP above 1.25, treat him as a liability for unders regardless of park factors — but validate against a larger sample before hardcoding (N=1 here).
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The Sasaki road/home split is a real and trackable signal, but one game is noise — flag for review after 3+ similar setups.

all_picks.case_against: Cantillo's 10.9% BB rate could lead to traffic on the bases, and if either bullpen falters, this game could get away from us. Both teams have been inconsistent, so there's variance risk.

all_picks.headline: Nick Martinez is pitching like an ace while Zac Gallen is imploding—the Trop does the rest.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Nick Martinez has been one of the best stories in baseball this year. His 2.73 ERA, 1.16 WHIP, and 0.81 HR/9 are all career-bests, and he's been especially good at home at Tropicana Field. The Trop is a pitcher's paradise with a 0.95 runs PF and 0.92 HR PF—it suppresses offense and rewards pitchers who can keep the ball in the park.

Zac Gallen, on the other hand, is having a nightmare season. His 6.10 ERA, 1.63 WHIP, and 1.48 HR/9 are all career-worsts. He's been giving up hard contact and hasn't been able to locate his secondary stuff. The Diamondbacks offense is decent (4.33 R/G), but they're facing a pitcher who's been unhittable at home.

The Rays at -137 is the play. I have them at 63% to win, which implies fair odds of -170. We're getting 33 cents of value on the moneyline.
all_picks.the_market: Rays opened -137 and have held steady. The total opened at 8.5 and has held as well. No sharp movement either way.
all_picks.weather_park: Tropicana Field is a dome—no weather concerns. Park factor is the edge: 0.95 runs, 0.92 HR.
all_picks.case_against: Gallen is a talented pitcher who could turn it around at any time. The Diamondbacks have some dangerous hitters (Corbin Carroll, Ketel Marte) who could get to Martinez if he's not sharp. The Rays offense has been inconsistent.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Martinez going 6+ innings with 5 or fewer hits allowed, the Rays offense scratching across 3-4 runs against a struggling Gallen, and the Trop doing its thing.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Gallen's ERA is higher than the Trop's attendance.

all_picks.headline: Reigning Cy Young winner at a pitcher-friendly park against a soft Reds lineup.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Paul Skenes is the reigning NL Cy Young winner and has rounded into form after a rough Opening Day. His 2.86 ERA, 0.93 WHIP, and 30.6% K rate are all elite, and he's been dominant at PNC Park (2.79 ERA at home). The Reds are starting Andrew Abbott, who has a 3.83 ERA but a concerning 10.7% BB rate—that's a red flag for any over, but it also means he's not going deep into games.

PNC Park is one of the best pitcher's parks in baseball with a 0.95 runs PF and 0.91 HR PF. The Reds offense has been mediocre (4.19 R/G), and they're facing the best pitcher they'll see all week. The Pirates offense isn't great either (5.05 R/G), but they don't need to be—Skenes will carry this game.

The under 7.5 at -105 is the cleanest play. I have this game at 6.8 runs, which gives us about 10 cents of edge on the under.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and has held. No movement, which suggests the market is comfortable with this number. We're getting the best price at BetMGM (-105).
all_picks.weather_park: PNC Park is outdoor but weather is favorable—72°F, light wind. Park factor is the story here: 0.95 runs, 0.91 HR.
all_picks.case_against: Abbott's 10.7% BB rate could lead to traffic on the bases, and if Skenes has an off night, the Reds could scratch across some runs. The Pirates bullpen has been inconsistent, so if Skenes exits early, the under could be in jeopardy.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Skenes going 7+ innings with 8+ strikeouts, Abbott keeping the Pirates offense in check for 5 innings, and both bullpens holding serve late.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Skenes at PNC Park is where offense goes to die.

autopsy.post_mortem: The under was dead by the end of the 2nd inning. Skenes — who entered 5-0 with a 0.53 ERA lifetime vs. Cincinnati — imploded in the second, surrendering four runs on RBI singles to Steer and Stephenson plus sacrifice flies from Dunn and De La Cruz, burning 38 pitches in that one frame alone and forcing an early exit after just five innings. The game was tied 4-4 when he left, but pinch-hitter Noelvi Marte broke it open with a 405-foot two-run homer off Mason Montgomery in the 8th, pushing the final to 6-4. The thesis leaned entirely on Skenes being Skenes, but ignored a critical red flag hiding in plain sight: he had posted a 4.03 ERA over his prior seven starts (0-5 record) and had already shown a pattern of second-inning blow-ups in his three 'bad' starts this season. The model treated his season ERA (2.86) as representative of his current form when his recent-start ERA told a very different story.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When a starter's rolling 7-start ERA (4.03) diverges from his season ERA (2.86) by more than 1.00 run, discount the season-long number and re-price the total using the recent-form ERA — N=1 loss on this specific pattern, insufficient to establish a rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One Skenes blow-up game does not confirm a regression pattern; watching for recurrence over next 5 starts before formalizing.

all_picks.headline: The best pitcher in baseball at home against a middling Cubs lineup—this is the floor play.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Jacob Misiorowski is having a historic season. His 1.45 ERA leads MLB, his 39.1% K rate is elite, and his 0.75 WHIP is absurd. He's thrown shutout innings in six of his last seven starts and just set a career-high with 14 strikeouts against the Phillies. The Cubs are starting Colin Rea, who has a 4.99 ERA, 1.37 HR/9, and an 8.1% BB rate—he's hittable and prone to the long ball.

The Brewers offense has been solid at home (5.22 R/G), and American Family Field is neutral for runs but slightly HR-boosting (1.06 PF). The Cubs have been inconsistent on the road, and their lineup doesn't have the firepower to keep up with Milwaukee's bats when Misiorowski is dealing. The run line at -109 is the sweet spot—we're getting plus-money value on a team that should win by 2+ runs more often than the market implies.

The market has Milwaukee at -235 on the moneyline, which implies about 70% win probability. I have them closer to 75% to win outright and 68% to cover -1.5. That's 8+ cents of edge on the run line, which is where we want to be.
all_picks.the_market: Brewers opened -235 and have held steady. The run line at -109 is where the value lives—public is hammering the ML but the run line hasn't moved.
all_picks.weather_park: American Family Field is a dome—no weather concerns. Park factor is neutral for runs (1.0) but slightly HR-boosting (1.06).
all_picks.case_against: Misiorowski is due for regression at some point—no one sustains a sub-2.00 ERA forever. If he has an off night and the Cubs get to him early, the Brewers bullpen will be tested. Rea has also been better on the road (3.81 ERA) than at home.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Misiorowski going 7+ innings with 10+ strikeouts, the Brewers offense providing 4+ runs of support, and the Cubs failing to generate any sustained offense against elite stuff.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Misiorowski is throwing 104 mph and the Cubs are bringing a knife to a gunfight.
all_picks.ladder_note: This is the ladder pick because Misiorowski's dominance creates the highest floor on the board. Even if the Brewers offense sputters, he's likely to keep the Cubs under 2 runs. The -109 price lands us right in the even-money band for the ladder challenge.

all_picks.headline: Citi Field suppresses homers, and neither offense has been consistent — the under has value.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Citi Field is one of the most pitcher-friendly parks in baseball for homers (0.91 PF). Both offenses have been inconsistent this season — the Mets are averaging 4.06 R/G and the Cubs 4.94 R/G, but neither has been explosive lately.

Freddy Peralta has struggled (4.83 ERA, 1.39 WHIP), but his 22.1 K% is still solid. Matthew Boyd has been worse (6.0 ERA), but his 31.0 K% is elite — he's missing bats even when he's getting hit. Both pitchers have the ability to string together a few good innings.

The total is set at 8.5, and the under is priced at -105 on DraftKings. That's a reasonable price for a game at a pitcher-friendly park with two inconsistent offenses.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 8.5 and has held steady. DraftKings and BetMGM both have the under at -105. No significant line movement.
all_picks.weather_park: Citi Field is HR-suppressing (0.91 PF). Outdoor game, but no significant weather concerns.
all_picks.case_against: Both pitchers have been inconsistent. If either one gets knocked around early, the bullpens will be tested — and both bullpens have had their struggles. The Cubs' offense can explode on any given night.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 5+ innings, Citi Field suppressing the long ball, and neither offense having a breakout game.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two mediocre starters at a pitcher's park. The under isn't sexy, but it's right.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Eight-game MLB-only slate with one dominant pitching matchup (Schlittler vs. Boston) and several prop angles. No NBA or NHL action today.
all_picks.executive_summary: Cam Schlittler is the story tonight — the Yankees' breakout ace takes a 1.71 ERA into Fenway against a depleted Red Sox lineup missing multiple infielders. The under at 8 is the cleanest play on the board, and Schlittler's K prop offers value against a Boston offense that's been striking out at an elevated clip. Cristopher Sánchez facing the MLB's highest-scoring offense in Washington creates a fascinating total, but the Nats' firepower makes the under too risky. The ladder rides with the Yankees-Red Sox Under 8 — two elite arms, a banged-up Boston lineup, and sharp money already on the low side.
all_picks.slate_analysis:
The board today is eight MLB games, no NBA or NHL playoff action. It's a mid-week slate with several pitching mismatches but limited data confidence across the pack — five of eight games came in below 0.5 on data confidence, which narrows the playable pool. The pricing climate is soft: most totals sit at 8-9, and the moneylines are clustered around pick'em territory except for the Phillies-Nationals and Yankees-Red Sox games where we see clear favorites.

The candidates that rose to the top: Yankees at Red Sox jumped off the page immediately — Cam Schlittler is pitching at a Cy Young level (1.71 ERA, 0.89 WHIP, 29.5 K%) and Boston's lineup is decimated by injuries (Story, Kiner-Falefa, Mayer all banged up or on IL). The under at 8 is the sharpest play on the board. Phillies at Nationals drew attention because Cristopher Sánchez (1.80 ERA, 28.5 K%) is elite, but Washington leads MLB in runs scored and their .331 xwOBA is fourth-best in baseball — that's a dangerous spot for an under. Cardinals-Diamondbacks looked interesting with McGreevy's 3.35 ERA against a struggling Gallen (6.10 ERA), but the data confidence was too low and the juice on STL ML pushed past -130. Giants-Athletics had a pitcher-friendly park signal but Jeffrey Springs' 2.3 HR/9 and the wind blowing out 8 mph muddied the picture. Mets-Cubs was a true coin flip at -108/-108 with neither pitcher inspiring confidence.

What got cut: The Phillies-Nationals under was tempting given Sánchez's dominance, but Washington's offense is legitimately elite — they lead MLB in runs scored and their underlying metrics support it. I can't back an under against the best-scoring team in baseball even with an ace on the mound. The Cardinals ML at -132 was close but the data confidence was 0.4 and Gallen, despite his struggles, has the pedigree to steal a game. The Giants-Athletics total was too murky with conflicting signals (pitcher-friendly park vs. wind out). The Tigers-Astros game had both pitchers with tiny sample sizes (Melton 31.2 IP, Imai 41 IP) and data confidence of 0.4.

The final six: Yankees-Red Sox Under 8 is the ladder pick — Schlittler is elite, Boston's lineup is depleted, and the total is priced at even money. Schlittler Ks Over 6.5 is a prop play that leverages his 29.5 K% against a Boston offense that's been striking out. Sánchez Ks Over 6.5 is a value play — he's averaging 7+ Ks per start and Cavalli's 23.5 K% suggests the Nats will swing and miss. The Cardinals ML at -132 gets in as a value play on McGreevy's solid season against a struggling Gallen. The Giants ML at -130 is a home favorite with the better pitcher in a pitcher-friendly park. The Mets-Cubs Under 8.5 rounds out the card — Citi Field suppresses homers and both offenses have been inconsistent.

all_picks.headline: Roupp's solid season at pitcher-friendly Oracle Park against a struggling Springs.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Landen Roupp has been a reliable arm for the Giants this season. His 4.15 ERA is league-average, but his 0.56 HR/9 is elite — and that matters at Oracle Park, which suppresses homers (0.85 PF). His 26.3 K% is solid, and he's been durable (80.1 IP).

Jeffrey Springs has struggled mightily. His 5.55 ERA and 2.3 HR/9 are both concerning, especially on the road against a Giants lineup that can do damage. The wind is blowing out 8 mph, which boosts HR probability by ~12% — that's bad news for a pitcher who's already giving up too many homers.

The Giants are at home, they have the better pitcher, and they're playing in a park that suppresses runs. The A's are a rebuilding team with a weak lineup.
all_picks.the_market: Giants opened as -130 favorites and have held steady. FanDuel has the best price at -130. No significant line movement.
all_picks.weather_park: Oracle Park is pitcher-friendly (0.93 runs PF, 0.85 HR PF). Wind blowing out 8 mph boosts HR ~12%, but the park effect still favors pitchers.
all_picks.case_against: The wind blowing out could help Springs' opponents — but it also helps the A's against Roupp. If Springs finds his command, the A's could steal one. The data confidence is lower than I'd like (0.45 in the pack).
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Roupp going 6+ innings at home, the Giants' offense doing enough against a struggling Springs, and Oracle Park suppressing the A's limited power.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Springs is giving up homers like he's running a charity. Oracle Park is not the place to fix that.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis was alive deep into the game. The Giants trailed 2-1 through five innings, then erupted for five runs in the sixth — Jung Hoo Lee's two-out, three-run triple off reliever Krook, followed immediately by Bericoto's two-run homer off Sterner — to take a 6-2 lead heading into the seventh. The Giants' bullpen then imploded across three straight innings: Langeliers singled in two in the 7th, McNeil singled in one in the 8th, and Kilian surrendered four runs with two outs in the 9th — Heim tied it, Butler put the A's ahead, and Muncy's bloop single plated two more to seal the 9-6 final. Roupp himself was not the problem; the loss was a classic multi-inning bullpen collapse after the Giants had already won the game.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a single bullpen collapse, not a systemic Giants relief failure pattern worth acting on yet.

all_picks.headline: McGreevy's solid season against a struggling Gallen — home favorite at a reasonable price.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Michael McGreevy has been quietly solid for the Cardinals this season. His 3.35 ERA and 1.15 WHIP are both above-average marks, and he's been durable (83.1 IP). His 15.9 K% is low, but he limits walks (6.0 BB%) and keeps the ball in the park (1.3 HR/9 at a HR-suppressing Busch Stadium).

Zac Gallen, on the other hand, has been a disaster. His 6.10 ERA is the worst of his career, and his 1.63 WHIP suggests the underlying numbers are just as bad as the surface stats. His 14.3 K% is a career-low, and his 1.48 HR/9 is a career-high. The Diamondbacks' ace has lost his ace status.

Busch Stadium suppresses homers (0.93 PF), which helps McGreevy's profile and hurts Gallen's. The Cardinals' offense (4.60 R/G) is better than Arizona's (4.33 R/G), and they're at home.
all_picks.the_market: Cardinals opened as -130 favorites and have held steady. FanDuel has the best price at -132. No significant line movement.
all_picks.weather_park: Busch Stadium is HR-suppressing (0.93 PF), which benefits McGreevy's groundball-heavy approach.
all_picks.case_against: Gallen has the pedigree to steal a game. If he finds his command, the Diamondbacks' lineup (Marte, Carroll, Perdomo) can do damage. McGreevy's low K% means he's relying on contact management — one bad inning can sink him.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: McGreevy going 6+ innings with 2-3 runs allowed, the Cardinals' offense doing enough against a struggling Gallen, and the home-field advantage mattering.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Gallen's having a nightmare season. McGreevy's having a quiet good one. Sometimes the boring play is the right play.

all_picks.headline: Sánchez's 28.5 K% and 7+ Ks per start make this plus-money line a value play.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Cristopher Sánchez is having a historic season. His 1.80 ERA ranks second in the NL, and his 28.5 K% is elite. He's averaging over 7 strikeouts per start and has logged a quality start in 9 of his last 10 outings. The line is set at 6.5, and DraftKings is offering +112 on the over.

The Nationals have the highest-scoring offense in baseball, but they also swing aggressively. Cade Cavalli, their starter, has a 23.5 K% — meaning the Nats' lineup is accustomed to striking out. Sánchez's changeup is one of the best pitches in baseball, and he's been generating whiffs at an elite rate all season.

Sánchez has a 2.27 ERA in eight career starts against Washington. He knows this lineup, and he's been dominant against them historically.
all_picks.the_market: DraftKings has the over at +112, FanDuel at -115, BetMGM at -135. The DK price is the clear value — plus money on a Cy Young candidate.
all_picks.weather_park: Nationals Park is neutral (1.01 runs PF). No weather concerns for K props.
all_picks.case_against: Washington's offense is elite. If they jump on Sánchez early and he has a short outing, the K total caps. His last start against the Brewers was his worst of the year (3 Ks in 5.2 IP).
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Sánchez pitching 6+ innings and maintaining his 28.5 K% against an aggressive Washington lineup.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: The Nats can hit. They can also strike out. Sánchez will make sure they do both.

autopsy.post_mortem: Sánchez's changeup command was off from pitch one — Washington jumped on him for four runs in the first inning, capped by a Curtis Mead solo homer, and added a fifth run in the third on a Jacob Young RBI single. Buried in a 5-0 hole with his pitch count climbing (92 pitches, 25 batters faced), Sánchez was pulled after just five innings — his shortest outing since April 7 — stranding the K prop at exactly 6, one short of the 6.5 line. The stuff was reportedly fine; the manager cited changeup command as the culprit, and two defensive errors (Realmuto throwing error, Bohm boot) burned extra pitches that further capped his inning total.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. Sánchez's season-long profile (7.1 K/start, 28.5 K%) remains intact — this was a command outlier on a single pitch (changeup) combined with a brutal first inning that compressed his outing, not a structural breakdown.

all_picks.headline: Schlittler's elite season meets a depleted Boston lineup — the under is the sharpest play on the board.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Cam Schlittler has emerged as the best pitcher in the American League. His 1.71 ERA is the lowest by a Yankees starter through 16 starts since Whitey Ford in 1964. His 0.89 WHIP ranks fourth in baseball, and his 29.5 K% means he's missing bats at an elite rate. He's coming off a 13-strikeout, zero-walk performance against the Reds — the kind of dominance that makes totals like this one feel like free money.

Boston's lineup is a shell of itself. Trevor Story is on the 60-day IL with a sports hernia. Isiah Kiner-Falefa hit the IL last week with forearm inflammation. Marcelo Mayer was scratched Tuesday with a sore foot and has been walking with a limp. The Red Sox are trotting out a patchwork infield against one of the best pitchers in baseball. Connelly Early is a solid arm (3.64 ERA, 22.5 K%) but his 1.55 HR/9 is a concern at Fenway — except Schlittler's ability to suppress runs on the other side makes this a one-sided pitching matchup.

The market agrees. The total opened at 8.5 on FanDuel and has been bet down to 8 across the board. Sharp money is on the under. Fenway's park factor for runs is 1.05 (slightly hitter-friendly), but that's offset by Schlittler's ability to generate weak contact — opponents are hitting .194 against him with an 87.9 mph average exit velocity.

all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 8.5 on FanDuel and has been bet down to 8 across all three books. DraftKings has the best price at -108. Sharp money is clearly on the under — the line movement tells the story.
all_picks.weather_park: Fenway Park is slightly hitter-friendly (1.05 runs PF), but Schlittler's elite contact suppression neutralizes the park effect.
all_picks.case_against: Fenway is still Fenway. Early's 1.55 HR/9 could bite him if the Yankees' power bats (Domínguez, Bellinger) catch one. If Schlittler has an off night — his first in months — this total could sail over.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Schlittler going 6+ innings with 7+ Ks, Boston's depleted lineup managing 2-3 runs max, and the Yankees' offense doing just enough against Early without exploding.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Boston's lineup looks like a MASH unit. Schlittler looks like a Cy Young. The math isn't hard.
all_picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board. Elite SP, depleted opposing lineup, sharp money support, even-money pricing. This is the ladder lock.

autopsy.post_mortem: Schlittler was dominant — 5 IP, 0 earned runs, 9 strikeouts — and the under thesis held on the pitching side. The game was blown open by a Yankees defensive catastrophe: New York committed a season-high four errors, and all six Boston runs were unearned. The decisive blow was a Caleb Durbin two-run homer in the fifth that scored on an error-extended inning, and the Yankees' bullpen couldn't hold a 4-3 deficit in the late innings. The under died not because the model was wrong about run suppression, but because a historically bad defensive performance manufactured six runs that had no business scoring.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The loss was a pure defensive variance event — four errors in a single game is an extreme outlier, not a modelable pattern.

all_picks.headline: Soriano's 3.03 ERA vs Gibson's disaster — Angels at home.
all_picks.the_thesis:
José Soriano has been one of the better stories in the Angels rotation this year. His 3.03 ERA is backed by a 25.5% K rate and a 0.98 HR/9 — he's missing bats and keeping the ball in the park. His 1.27 WHIP is slightly elevated due to a 12.0% walk rate, but he's been effective despite the free passes.

Trey Gibson is a disaster waiting to happen. His 5.81 ERA is backed by a 1.71 WHIP and a 13.9% walk rate — he's putting runners on base constantly. His 16.4% K rate means he's not missing bats, and his 1.38 HR/9 means the damage comes in bunches. In just 26.1 innings, he's been a liability.

The Angels are -122 favorites at home, which feels about right given the pitching disparity. Angel Stadium is neutral (0.99 runs PF), so neither team gets a park boost. The Orioles offense (4.64 R/G) is solid, but Gibson's inability to throw strikes will put runners on base for Soriano to strand.
all_picks.the_market: Angels opened as -122 favorites and the line has held. FanDuel offers the best price. The market is pricing LA at roughly 53% implied, which feels low given Gibson's profile.
all_picks.weather_park: Angel Stadium is neutral for runs (0.99 PF) and HRs (1.01 PF). No weather concerns.
all_picks.case_against: Soriano's 12.0% walk rate is concerning — he's putting runners on base too. The Orioles have a deep lineup that can capitalize on mistakes. Gibson has only 26.1 IP this season, so the sample is small and he could turn in a decent outing.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Soriano going 5+ innings with 3 or fewer runs, Gibson walking himself into trouble, and the Angels offense capitalizing on free passes.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Gibson's walk rate is a cry for help. Soriano just needs to be average to win this.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Sixteen-game MLB slate with no NBA or NHL action. Two elite pitching matchups stand out — Ohtani vs Ryan in Minnesota and Skubal vs Weathers in Detroit — plus several exploitable prop markets at Coors Field and in the high-total games.
all_picks.executive_summary: The board today is all MLB, and the two marquee pitching matchups are where the edge lives. Ohtani at Target Field against Joe Ryan is a clash of aces with the total sitting at 8.0 — both starters have sub-3.00 ERAs and elite command, and the park suppresses runs. In Detroit, Skubal returns from his elbow procedure looking sharp, facing a Yankees lineup that's potent but travels to a pitcher-friendly Comerica. The Coors Field game screams over with Kyle Freeland's 7.36 ERA hosting Ranger Suarez, but the real play is the total bases prop slate against Freeland's 1.91 HR/9. The ladder rides with the Twins-Dodgers under — two elite arms, neutral park, lowest total on the board.
all_picks.slate_analysis:
Today's board features sixteen MLB games spread across the afternoon and evening, with first pitches ranging from 1:10 PM ET to 9:45 PM ET. No NBA or NHL games are in scope, so we're working exclusively with baseball sides, totals, and props. The pricing climate is relatively tight — most moneylines sit between -150 and +150, with only a handful of games showing significant separation between favorites and dogs.

The top candidates that earned hard looks: (1) Twins-Dodgers under 8.0 — Ohtani's 1.47 ERA and 0.88 WHIP against Ryan's 2.99 ERA and 1.00 WHIP at a neutral Target Field is the cleanest pitching matchup on the board; (2) Tigers ML vs Yankees — Skubal back from his NanoScope procedure, 3.02 ERA and elite 3.8% BB rate, facing Weathers who has a 1.8 HR/9; (3) Red Sox-Rockies over 10.5 — Freeland's 7.36 ERA and 1.91 HR/9 at Coors against Suarez is a run-scoring environment; (4) Pirates-Mariners under 7.5 — Ashcraft (3.18 ERA, 0.80 HR/9) vs Woo (3.94 ERA, 0.91 HR/9) at HR-suppressing PNC Park; (5) Coors total bases props — Freeland's HR/9 makes XBH props attractive; (6) Angels ML vs Orioles — Soriano's 3.03 ERA vs Gibson's 5.81 ERA and 1.71 WHIP.

What got cut: The Cubs-Mets game (data_confidence 0.4, lineups not confirmed for Game 1 of doubleheader). The White Sox-Guardians game has both teams near pick'em pricing but neither SP profile creates separation — Fedde's 15.6% K rate is too low to trust, and Bibee's 1.62 HR/9 at a HR-boosting park creates variance. The Brewers-Reds game has Drohan vs Lowder, but Lowder's 12.1% BB rate and the HR-boosting GABP make the under untenable and the over already priced in. The Padres-Braves game has incomplete SP data for JP Sears. The Cardinals-Diamondbacks game has no data for Mitch Bratt.

The final six picks: (1) Twins-Dodgers Under 8.0 — the ladder pick, two elite arms, lowest total on the board, neutral park; (2) Tigers ML — Skubal's return against a beatable Weathers; (3) Red Sox -1.5 at Coors — Suarez's 2.93 ERA and 0.47 HR/9 against Freeland's disaster profile; (4) Pirates-Mariners Under 7.5 — two quality arms at PNC Park; (5) Angels ML — Soriano's elite profile vs Gibson's 5.81 ERA; (6) Ceddanne Rafaela Total Bases Over 1.5 at Coors — Freeland's 1.91 HR/9 and the Coors environment make XBH likely for a hitter in the top of the order.

all_picks.headline: Two quality arms at HR-suppressing PNC Park — the under is plus money.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Braxton Ashcraft has emerged as one of the better young arms in baseball. His 3.18 ERA is backed by a 0.80 HR/9 and a 1.10 WHIP — he's not giving up free passes (6.0% BB rate) and he's not giving up home runs. At PNC Park, which already suppresses HRs (0.91 PF), Ashcraft's profile becomes even more valuable.

Bryan Woo counters with a 3.94 ERA and an elite 4.5% walk rate — the best command in the Mariners rotation. His 0.91 HR/9 is solid, and his 1.00 WHIP suggests he's keeping baserunners to a minimum. The Mariners offense (4.11 R/G) is below-average, which helps the under case.

The total is 7.5-8.0 across books, with FanDuel offering the under at +102 on 7.5. That's plus money on a game featuring two quality starters at a pitcher-friendly park. The Pirates score 4.97 R/G at home, but that's inflated by some blowouts — their median game is closer to 4 runs.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5-8.0 and has held steady. FanDuel is offering the under 7.5 at +102, while DraftKings and BetMGM have moved to 8.0. The plus money at FanDuel is the play.
all_picks.weather_park: PNC Park is pitcher-friendly (0.95 runs PF, 0.91 HR PF). No weather concerns for this evening game.
all_picks.case_against: The Pirates offense has been hot lately (4.97 R/G), and they're at home where they hit better. Woo has been inconsistent — his 3.94 ERA is fine but not elite. If either starter exits early, both bullpens have had their struggles.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6+ innings, combined K rate suppressing contact, and the HR-suppressing park keeping balls in the yard.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two arms that don't walk guys, a park that eats fly balls, and we're getting plus money. The market is asleep.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis was built around two quality starters neutralizing offenses — but it was Bryan Woo who collapsed, not Ashcraft. Pittsburgh sent nine men to the plate in a five-run fourth inning against Woo, going ahead 5-1 on Endy Rodríguez's two-run double, a Tyler Callihan triple, and back-to-back RBI singles; a four-run seventh pushed it to 9-1 before garbage-time bullpen runs finished the 11-1 final. Woo had shown a pattern of extreme volatility entering this start — two scoreless seven-inning gems sandwiched around two outings where he allowed 12 runs over 11.1 combined innings — and the pick leaned on his 3.94 ERA and elite walk rate without adequately weighting his ceiling/floor spread. The under was dead by the fourth inning; this was not a late-game collapse or a freak event.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Before backing an under that hinges on a specific starter's ERA, cross-check his last 4-6 starts for variance in runs allowed — if the standard deviation is high (e.g., alternating shutdowns and blowups), discount the ERA and widen the projected run range accordingly; N=1 loss, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one Woo blowup does not confirm a systemic model gap, but the volatility signal was visible in the pre-game data and was not weighted.

all_picks.headline: Suarez's elite HR suppression meets Freeland's disaster profile at Coors.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Kyle Freeland has been a disaster in 2026. His 7.36 ERA is backed by a 1.91 HR/9 — at Coors Field, where the ball already carries, that's a recipe for crooked numbers. His 1.58 WHIP means baserunners are constant, and his 18.7% K rate means he's not missing bats. The Rockies are essentially punting this start.

Ranger Suarez, now with Boston after signing a 5-year, $130M deal, has been excellent. His 2.93 ERA and 0.47 HR/9 are elite — he's allowed just 4 home runs in 76.2 innings. At Coors, where fly balls become souvenirs, Suarez's ground-ball tendencies and HR suppression become even more valuable. His 1.12 WHIP and 24.2% K rate suggest he can navigate the altitude.

The Red Sox are -164 on the moneyline, which is too juicy for a straight bet. But the run line at -110 offers value — Boston should win this game by multiple runs given the pitching disparity. Coors inflates totals, but it also inflates margins when one team has a significant pitching advantage.
all_picks.the_market: Red Sox opened as -164 favorites and the line has held. The run line at -110 on FanDuel offers better value than the moneyline. Total is 10.5-11.0 across books, reflecting the Coors environment.
all_picks.weather_park: Coors Field is the most hitter-friendly park in baseball (1.18 runs PF, 1.14 HR PF). Temperature is 83°F with light wind — standard Coors conditions that favor offense.
all_picks.case_against: Coors Field is unpredictable. Even bad pitchers can have good days at altitude, and even good pitchers can get shelled. Suarez has never pitched at Coors in his career, so there's some unknown about how he'll handle the environment. The Rockies offense (4.51 R/G) can put up runs at home.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Suarez going 5+ innings with 3 or fewer runs, Freeland getting knocked around early, and the Red Sox offense capitalizing on Coors conditions.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Freeland's HR/9 at Coors is a war crime. Suarez doesn't give up homers. This isn't complicated.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis executed almost perfectly through six innings — Suarez was dominant (9 K, 1 walk, 13 swing-and-misses, retired the final 7 batters in order) and Boston led 6-3 heading to the seventh. The game unraveled entirely in the Boston bullpen: Marcelo Mayer fumbled a routine grounder with two outs in the seventh, keeping the inning alive, and three consecutive RBI singles off Tyron Guerrero and Danny Coulombe tied it at 6-6. Then in the eighth, with runners on first and third off Justin Slaten (0-4), Tyler Freeman dropped a walk-off bunt single to plate the go-ahead run, and Colorado added an insurance run to seal the 8-6 win. A mid-game injury to 3B Caleb Durbin (left pinkie subluxation sliding into first) also forced a defensive reshuffle that contributed to the Seigler throwing error that let Colorado back in during the third.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this was a clean bullpen/error collapse after the starting pitching thesis fully validated; no model adjustment warranted.

all_picks.headline: Skubal back and dealing, Weathers gives up too many homers — Tigers at home.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Tarik Skubal's return from his NanoScope elbow procedure has been nothing short of remarkable. The two-time Cy Young winner underwent surgery on May 6 and was back on a major league mound by June 13 — far ahead of the typical 2-3 month timeline. In his starts since returning, he's looked like himself: a 3.02 ERA, 1.02 WHIP, and an absurd 3.8% walk rate that ranks among the best in baseball. His 26.9% K rate means he's still missing bats.

Ryan Weathers has been serviceable for the Yankees but his 1.8 HR/9 is a glaring weakness. Comerica Park suppresses home runs (0.95 HR PF), which helps, but Weathers has allowed fly balls to leave the yard at an alarming rate all season. His 4.13 ERA is fine, but the underlying metrics suggest he's been lucky — that HR/9 will regress in the wrong direction eventually.

The Tigers offense isn't explosive (4.08 R/G), but they don't need to be with Skubal on the mound. The Yankees score 5.08 R/G, but Skubal's elite command means they won't get free passes. Detroit at home with their ace is the right side.
all_picks.the_market: Tigers opened as -132 favorites and the line has held steady. DraftKings offers the best price at -132. The market is pricing Detroit at roughly 55% implied, which feels about right given the pitching matchup, but I have them closer to 60%.
all_picks.weather_park: Comerica Park is neutral for runs (0.96 PF) and slightly suppresses HRs (0.95 PF). No weather concerns for this evening game.
all_picks.case_against: The Yankees lineup is dangerous, and Skubal is still building back up after his procedure. If he's on a soft pitch count (80-85 pitches), the Tigers bullpen would need to cover 3+ innings against a potent offense. Detroit's offense has also been inconsistent, ranking in the bottom third of the league.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Skubal going 6+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, Weathers giving up at least one HR, and the Tigers bullpen holding a lead.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: The Cy Young winner is back, the park eats fly balls, and Weathers can't stop serving them up. Math is math.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis leaned on Skubal's season-long ERA (3.02) without adequately discounting that he was still rounding back into form post-surgery — Bless You Boys noted he carried a 4.35 ERA and 5.42 FIP across his first two post-IL starts, with three home runs already allowed in that stretch. Tonight made it worse: Goldschmidt took him deep in the 1st inning on a 3-1 fastball, then again in the 3rd, and Domínguez hit a go-ahead two-run shot in the 6th — Skubal tied a career high with three home runs allowed, his most since 2021. The 'case against' explicitly flagged a soft pitch count and bullpen exposure, but the deeper flaw was treating the season ERA as representative of a pitcher who was clearly not yet himself. Weathers, meanwhile, held Detroit to two runs on six hits over six innings — the thesis that his HR/9 would hurt him at Comerica never materialized.
autopsy.candidate_rule: When a returning-from-injury starter's last 2+ starts show a FIP 1.5+ runs above his season ERA, regress projected performance toward the recent sample, not the season line — do not back him as a heavy favorite until he posts at least 3 post-IL starts with sub-4.00 FIP. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule.)
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one start does not confirm a pattern, but the pre-game data signal (4.35 ERA, 5.42 FIP in post-IL starts) was available and underweighted.

all_picks.headline: Two aces, lowest total on the board, neutral park — this is the floor play.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Shohei Ohtani has been otherworldly on the mound in 2026. His 1.47 ERA through 73.2 innings is backed by a 0.88 WHIP and a minuscule 0.37 HR/9 — that's elite run prevention by any measure. He's striking out 27% of batters while walking just 7.6%, which means he's not putting runners on base and he's not giving up free passes. When Ohtani is on, he dominates, and the Dodgers have been riding him as a true ace.

Joe Ryan counters with his own strong profile — a 2.99 ERA, 1.00 WHIP, and a 28.1% K rate against just 5.1% walks. Ryan was scratched from his last start due to illness but earned a win against the Rangers on June 19, so he's healthy and in rhythm. Target Field plays neutral (0.98 runs PF, 0.97 HR PF), which means neither offense gets a boost from the environment.

The market has this at 8.0, the lowest total on the entire board. That's appropriate given the pitching matchup, but the de-vigged implied probability sits around 52-53% for the under. I have this closer to 62% — both starters have the command and stuff to go 6+ innings with minimal damage, and neither bullpen is a liability. The Twins offense has been solid (4.84 R/G) but faces a pitcher who's allowed just 3 HR all season. The Dodgers score 5.24 R/G but Ryan's elite walk rate means they won't get free baserunners.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 8.0 and hasn't moved across all three books. The under is -105 at FanDuel and DraftKings, -110 at BetMGM. No significant line movement suggests sharp money hasn't piled on either side yet, but the number is appropriately low given the arms involved.
all_picks.weather_park: Target Field is neutral for runs (0.98 PF) and slightly suppresses HRs (0.97 PF). Weather is not a factor for this evening game.
all_picks.case_against: The Dodgers offense is legitimately dangerous — 5.24 R/G is second-best in baseball. If Ryan doesn't have his best stuff coming off illness, LA could put up a crooked number early. Ohtani has also been managed carefully this year, so a short outing (5 IP) would expose the Twins bullpen to the Dodgers' deep lineup.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6+ innings with 2 or fewer runs allowed, combined K rate suppressing contact, and neither bullpen imploding in the late innings.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two aces, one total, zero drama. The sharps haven't moved this number because there's nowhere for it to go.
all_picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board — two elite SPs with sub-3.00 ERAs, neutral park, lowest total available. This is the highest-confidence play at near even money.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Sixteen-game MLB slate with no NBA or NHL action. Two elite pitching matchups stand out — Ohtani vs Ryan in Minnesota and Skubal vs Weathers in Detroit — plus several exploitable prop markets at Coors Field and in the high-total games.
