Data confidence is frustratingly thin across the board: only CIN-NYM has three-book coverage, and most games are sitting at 0.40-0.45 confidence scores. That said, the pitching matchups are readable, and the line movement tells a story.

The candidates that rose: Chase Burns at home vs the Mets jumped off the page immediately — 2.14 ERA, 29.6% K rate, and a K prop line of 7.5 that feels soft given his recent form. The Houston-Detroit total dropped a full run from 9.0 to 8.0 since open, which is sharp money screaming under. Wheeler's return to the Phillies rotation against a rookie Marlins arm (Ryan Gusto, 9.0 IP on the season) offers run-line value at +128. The Cubs-Rockies game has weather boosting HR by 15% and Lorenzen on the mound for Colorado — that's an over lean. The Dodgers-Rays total dropped from 9.5 to 8.5 with Lauer regressing hard (6.69 L3 ERA) — under consideration there too. The Reds ML at -142 against Myers (1.63 HR/9) in a HR-friendly park also got a hard look.

What got cut: The Phillies ML at -174 exceeds our -150 juice cap for straight bets, so we pivoted to the run line at +128. The Cubs ML at -190 is also too juiced. The STL-SD game is a pure coin flip with no edge — both MLs near pick'em, no line movement, no weather factor. The ARI-LAA game has Ureña's 13.6% BB rate creating chaos that's hard to model. The ATH-PIT game has a 10.5 total that's already baked in the offensive environment — no edge either way. The WSH-KC game has Mitch Spence on the mound with a 23.8% BB rate and 13.5 ERA in 4.0 IP — that's a stay-away, not a play.

The final six: Burns K Under 7.5 is the ladder pick — best floor, clean matchup, plus-money price. Houston-Detroit Under 8.0 rides the sharp money and a full-run drop. Phillies -1.5 at +128 backs Wheeler's return against a rookie. Cubs-Rockies Over 9.5 leans into weather and Lorenzen's struggles. Dodgers-Rays Under 8.5 follows the line movement and Lauer's regression. Reds ML at -142 backs Burns at home in a HR-friendly park against a flyball-prone Myers. Six plays, all grounded in pitching edges, line movement, or weather.

autopsy.post_mortem: Both starters replicated their June 10 form almost exactly: Lorenzen held the Cubs to 1 run over 5 innings (same as his Coors outing), and Imanaga allowed 1 run over 5⅔ innings. The wind blowing out did produce a 434-foot Crow-Armstrong leadoff homer in the first, but that was the extent of the weather dividend — the Cubs went 0-for-9 with RISP through 8 innings, and the game finished 5-4 with just 9 total runs. The thesis leaned entirely on Lorenzen's season-long ERA (7.54) without accounting for the critical DATA point hiding in plain sight: his June 10 start against these same Cubs — 1 ER, 7 K, 5 IP — was a direct preview of what he'd do in the rematch, and the market had already priced that in.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Before betting an Over on a pitching rematch scheduled within 7 days of the first meeting, check the starter's most recent line against that specific opponent — if it diverges sharply from season ERA, weight the recent matchup line more heavily than the season number; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — one rematch spot does not confirm a pattern.

all_picks.headline: Wheeler's back, and he's facing a rookie with 9 IP on the season.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Zack Wheeler returned from thoracic outlet surgery and has been dominant — 2.22 ERA, 0.85 WHIP, 24.9% K rate through 56.2 IP. He's pitching at home against Ryan Gusto, a Marlins rookie with exactly 9.0 innings of MLB experience and a 6.00 ERA. This is a mismatch on paper, and the run line at +128 offers value because the Phillies ML at -174 exceeds our juice cap.

The Phillies offense isn't elite (4.01 R/G), but they don't need to be against Gusto. Wheeler will keep Miami's offense (4.31 R/G) in check, and the Phillies bullpen should close it out. Citizens Bank Park has a 1.10 HR park factor, which favors the home team's power bats.

The total rose from 8.0 to 8.5 since open, which suggests the market expects some offense — but that's likely coming from the Phillies side against the rookie. At +128, we're getting plus money on a team that should win by 2+ runs more often than the line implies.
all_picks.the_market: Phillies ML at -174 (too juiced), run line at +128. Total rose from 8.0 to 8.5 since open. Market expects offense, likely from Phillies side.
all_picks.weather_park: Citizens Bank Park, 78°F, wind 7 mph out to CF. HR-friendly conditions favor the home team.
all_picks.case_against: Wheeler is still building up after surgery — his L3 IP/start is 18.73, which means he may not go deep. If he exits after 5 innings, the Phillies bullpen has to hold a lead. Gusto could also surprise — small samples cut both ways.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Wheeler dominating for 5-6 innings, Phillies offense tagging the rookie early, and a 2+ run win.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Wheeler's back. The rookie's not ready. Math is math.
all_picks.slate_assessment: MLB-only Monday with 10 games, no NBA or NHL action after both championship series concluded over the weekend. Thin data confidence across the board (most games at 0.40-0.45), but several pitching matchups and one strong K prop stand out.

all_picks.headline: Full-run drop from open, sharp money screaming under in Houston.
all_picks.the_thesis:
This total opened at 9.0 and has dropped a full run to 8.0 — that's not public money, that's sharp action. The market is telling us something: both offenses are suppressed tonight. Troy Melton for Detroit has been quietly effective — 2.81 ERA, 1.01 WHIP, and a 13.7% K rate that suggests he's pitching to contact and getting outs. Kai-Wei Teng for Houston has a 3.71 ERA but a concerning 11.2% BB rate that could create traffic.

The key here is the line movement. A full-run drop in a game with two mid-tier starters means the sharps see something in the matchup — likely that both offenses are cold or that the bullpens are fresh. Detroit's offense has been inconsistent (4.06 R/G), and Houston's 4.56 R/G is inflated by a few blowouts. Daikin Park is assumed neutral, so no park factor to worry about.

At -118, we're getting a fair price on a total that's already been hammered down by sharp money. The market has done the work for us.

the_data.label: Teng BB%
the_data.context: Walks are a concern, but limits damage

all_picks.the_market: Opened at 9.0, now at 8.0 across all books. That's a full-run drop — significant sharp action on the under. Houston ML also shortened 4.5pp since open, suggesting sharps like the home team in a low-scoring game.
all_picks.weather_park: Daikin Park is a retractable-roof stadium, assumed neutral. No weather impact.
all_picks.case_against: Teng's 11.2% BB rate could load the bases and lead to crooked innings. If either bullpen falters, this could sail over. The small sample on Melton (25.2 IP) is a concern — we don't really know what he is yet.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 5+ innings, bullpens holding, and the sharp money being right about this matchup.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: The sharps moved this a full run. We're not smarter than them.

autopsy.post_mortem: The entire thesis was built around Troy Melton starting — his 2.81 ERA, 1.01 WHIP, and ability to pitch to contact were the primary run-suppression argument. Melton was scratched with back tightness before first pitch, and Drew Anderson opened a bullpen game in his place, lasting just 2.2 innings. With the pitching anchor gone, Colt Keith went off for three home runs and six RBI, and Detroit rolled to a 9-3 final — a 12-run total that obliterated the Under 8.0. The pick was dead the moment Melton's scratch was announced; the model never got a fair test.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Flag any total pick where the named starting pitcher accounts for >50% of the run-suppression thesis — require a re-evaluation trigger if that SP is scratched within 3 hours of first pitch before locking the bet; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the failure mode (SP scratch invalidating a total pick) is a known, recurring risk worth monitoring across the full season sample.

all_picks.headline: Mize returns from the IL on a workload cap — 55 pitches in his rehab start, expected to go 4-5 innings max.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Casey Mize is returning from the IL today after missing two weeks with right adductor inflammation. He threw 55 pitches in a rehab start with Single-A Lakeland on Tuesday — 5 innings, 1 run, 5 Ks. Manager A.J. Hinch confirmed he won't pitch deep into the game. The workload cap is real.

Before the injury, Mize was excellent: 2.27 ERA, 0.97 WHIP, 26.5% K rate across 47.2 innings. But 26.5% K rate over 4-5 innings is 4-5 Ks max. If he's on a 70-75 pitch cap (which is likely given the 55-pitch rehab start), he's not going deep enough to rack up Ks.

The Guardians offense is also patient. They've been one of the better contact teams in the AL, and they're not going to swing at everything Mize throws. If Mize goes 4.2 innings and exits with 4 Ks, the under cashes.
all_picks.the_market: Line is 4.5 at FanDuel with -110 on the under. The market is pricing Mize for 4-5 Ks, which aligns with the workload cap. No movement on this line.
all_picks.weather_park: Progressive Field is neutral. Weather is benign — no impact on K rate.
all_picks.case_against: Mize's K rate before the injury was 26.5% — if he goes 5 innings, that's 5-6 Ks. If the Guardians swing early in counts and Mize gets ahead, he could rack up Ks quickly. The workload cap is also uncertain — Hinch could let him go 80+ pitches if he's cruising.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Mize going 4-5 innings on a workload cap and exiting with 4 or fewer Ks.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Mize threw 55 pitches in his rehab start. He's not going 7 innings today.
all_picks.slate_assessment: 15-game MLB board plus Stanley Cup Final Game 5 — two elite pitching matchups (Sánchez/Harrison in Milwaukee, Williams/Mize in Cleveland) anchor the slate, with weather-driven totals and a handful of prop edges rounding out the card.
all_picks.executive_summary: The board today is anchored by two elite pitching matchups: Cristopher Sánchez — who's thrown 50⅔ consecutive scoreless innings and owns a 1.54 ERA — faces Kyle Harrison in Milwaukee, while Gavin Williams (99 Ks, 3.32 ERA) duels a returning Casey Mize in Cleveland. Both games scream under. The Phillies-Brewers Under 6.5 is the ladder pick — two lefty aces in a dome, both offenses neutral vs LHP, and the total already squeezed to 6.5. We're also riding Mize's workload cap in Cleveland, a weather-suppressed under in Kansas City, and a couple of prop plays where the data lines up cleanly.
all_picks.slate_analysis:
Today's board features 15 MLB games and Game 5 of the Stanley Cup Final between Carolina and Vegas. The NHL game is priced at a pick'em with the total at 5.5/6 — tight, but the series is tied 2-2 after Carolina's road win in Game 4, and the data confidence is too thin to build a strong case either way. On the MLB side, the pricing climate is soft: most games are near pick'em or short favorites, and several totals are inflated by weather signals (wind out at GABP, Target Field, Guaranteed Rate, and Las Vegas Ballpark). The highest-total games are in Las Vegas (13.5-14) and Cincinnati (10), but the pitching matchups there are messy.

The top candidates that rose during my review: (1) PHI @ MIL Under 6.5 — Sánchez vs Harrison, two elite lefties, indoor park, both offenses neutral vs LHP. (2) DET @ CLE Under 7 — Williams vs Mize, both high-K arms, Mize on a workload cap after returning from the IL, neutral weather. (3) HOU @ KC Under 8.5 — Arrighetti vs Kolek, wind blowing in 11 mph, HR-suppressing park, both offenses middling. (4) ATL @ NYM Under 8.5 — Elder vs Peralta, Citi Field HR-suppressing, but weather is blowing out 13 mph and boosting HR ~19%, which muddies the under case. (5) Gavin Williams Ks Over 5.5 — Williams leads the AL with 99 Ks, 28.7% K rate, facing a Tigers lineup that's been without key bats. (6) Casey Mize Ks Under — Mize is returning from the IL with a 55-pitch rehab start; workload cap is real.

What got cut: ATL @ NYM Under — the weather signal (+23% HR impact) directly contradicts the under thesis; I can't back an under when the wind is blowing out that hard at Citi Field. The Mets-Braves total is a pass. SD @ BAL — data confidence is 0.45, Rogers has a 6.15 ERA, and the weather is boosting HR ~20%; too much variance. NYY @ TOR — no props, data confidence 0.4, Corbin vs Warren is a coin flip. The NHL game — data confidence 0.35, no strong edge on either side or the total.

The final six picks: (1) PHI @ MIL Under 6.5 — ladder pick, best floor on the board. (2) DET @ CLE Under 7 — two high-K arms, Mize on a workload cap. (3) HOU @ KC Under 8.5 — wind in, HR-suppressing park, both SPs solid. (4) Gavin Williams Ks Over 5.5 — 99 Ks on the year, 28.7% K rate, Tigers lineup depleted. (5) Casey Mize Ks Under 4.5 — workload cap after IL return, 55 pitches in rehab start. (6) Cristopher Sánchez Ks Over 6.5 — 113 Ks in 93.1 IP, 30.1% K rate, Brewers offense neutral vs LHP.

all_picks.headline: Wind blowing in 11 mph at Kauffman — HR-suppressing park, both SPs solid, offenses middling.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Kauffman Stadium is already one of the most HR-suppressing parks in baseball (0.92 HR PF), and today the wind is blowing in from left field at 11 mph — that's an additional 14% HR suppression on top of the park factor. In a game where both offenses are middling (Royals 4.0 R/G, Astros 4.62 R/G), the weather is actively working against the over.

Spencer Arrighetti has been excellent for Houston: 2.21 ERA, 1.19 WHIP, 21.8% K rate across 57 innings. His BB/9 is elevated at 12.8%, but his HR/9 is just 0.47 — elite. He's not giving up the long ball, and in a park where the wind is blowing in, that's even more valuable. Stephen Kolek has been solid for Kansas City: 3.14 ERA, 1.07 WHIP, 17.4% K rate across 43 innings. His HR/9 is 1.05 — slightly elevated but manageable in this park.

The total opened at 8.5 and sits at 8.5 across all three books. The market is pricing this as a 4-4 or 5-3 game, and I agree. With the wind blowing in and both SPs solid, this is a clean under spot.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 8.5 and sits at 8.5 across all three books. All three books are offering -102 on the under — that's near even money on a game with HR-suppressing conditions. The market hasn't moved, which suggests sharp money is comfortable with this number.
all_picks.weather_park: Kauffman Stadium is HR-suppressing (0.92 HR PF). Wind is blowing in from left field at 11 mph, adding another 14% HR suppression.
all_picks.case_against: Arrighetti's BB/9 is 12.8% — that's a lot of free baserunners. If he walks the leadoff man in a few innings, the Royals could push runs across without needing the long ball. Kolek's sample size is also small (43 IP) — we don't have a full picture of who he is yet.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 5+ innings, the wind suppressing HR, and the bullpens holding serve in a low-scoring game.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Wind blowing in at Kauffman. The over needs a miracle. We don't believe in miracles.

all_picks.headline: Two high-K arms in a pitcher's park — Williams leads the AL with 99 Ks; Mize returns from the IL on a workload cap.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Gavin Williams has been one of the best pitchers in the American League this season. He's 9-3 with a 3.32 ERA and 99 strikeouts in 86.2 innings — that's a 28.7% K rate, which means he's punching out more than a quarter of the batters he faces. He leads the AL in strikeouts and has fanned 6+ in 10 of his 14 starts. His WHIP is 1.10, his BB/9 is 8.1% — slightly elevated but manageable.

Casey Mize is returning from the IL today after missing two weeks with right adductor inflammation. He threw 55 pitches in a rehab start on Tuesday and is expected to be on a workload cap — manager A.J. Hinch confirmed he won't pitch deep into the game. Before the injury, Mize was excellent: 2.27 ERA, 0.97 WHIP, 26.5% K rate across 47.2 innings. But the workload cap is real, and the Tigers bullpen will be involved early.

Progressive Field is a neutral park (0.97 runs PF, 0.96 HR PF), and the weather is benign — 74°F, wind blowing left to right, no HR impact. Both offenses are middling: the Guardians score 4.01 R/G, the Tigers score 4.06 R/G. This is a 3-2 or 4-3 game.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 7 and sits at 7 across all three books. BetMGM is offering +100 on the under — even money on a game with two high-K arms and a workload cap on one starter. The market hasn't moved, which suggests sharp money is comfortable with this number.
all_picks.weather_park: Progressive Field is neutral (0.97 runs PF). Weather is 74°F with wind blowing left to right — no HR impact.
all_picks.case_against: Mize's workload cap means the Tigers bullpen will be involved early. If the Guardians get to the pen in the 5th or 6th inning, they could push runs across. Williams has also allowed 2+ HR in 3 of his last 5 starts — his HR/9 is 1.36, which is elevated. One big swing could flip the game.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Williams going 6+ innings with 6+ Ks, Mize going 4-5 innings before the cap kicks in, and both bullpens holding serve in a low-scoring game.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Mize is on a pitch count. Williams is on a heater. The under is on the board.

The top candidates that rose during my review: (1) PHI @ MIL Under 6.5 — Sánchez vs Harrison, two elite lefties, indoor park, both offenses neutral vs LHP. (2) DET @ CLE Under 7 — Williams vs Mize, both high-K arms, Mize on a workload cap after returning from the IL, neutral weather. (3) HOU @ KC Under 8.5 — Arrighetti vs Kolek, wind blowing in 11 mph, HR-suppressing park, both offenses middling. (4) ATL @ NYM Under 8.5 — Elder vs Peralta, Citi Field HR-suppressing, but weather is blowing out 13 mph and boosting HR ~19%, which muddies the under case. (5) Gavin Williams Ks Over 5.5 — Williams leads the AL with 99 Ks, 28.7% K rate, facing a Tigers lineup that's been without key bats. (6) Casey Mize Ks Under — Mize is returning from the IL with a 55-pitch rehab start; workload cap is real.

What got cut: ATL @ NYM Under — the weather signal (+23% HR impact) directly contradicts the under thesis; I can't back an under when the wind is blowing out that hard at Citi Field. The Mets-Braves total is a pass. SD @ BAL — data confidence is 0.45, Rogers has a 6.15 ERA, and the weather is boosting HR ~20%; too much variance. (3) HOU @ KC Under 8.5 — wind in, HR-suppressing park, both SPs solid. (4) Gavin Williams Ks Over 5.5 — 99 Ks on the year, 28.7% K rate, Tigers lineup depleted. (5) Casey Mize Ks Under 4.5 — workload cap after IL return, 55 pitches in rehab start. (6) Cristopher Sánchez Ks Over 6.5 — 113 Ks in 93.1 IP, 30.1% K rate, Brewers offense neutral vs LHP.

all_picks.headline: Two elite lefties in a dome — Sánchez owns a 1.54 ERA and 50⅔ scoreless innings; Harrison's K rate is 31.3%.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Cristopher Sánchez has been the best pitcher in baseball this season. Through 14 starts, he's 8-2 with a 1.54 ERA and 113 strikeouts across 93.1 innings — that's a 30.1% K rate, which means he's punching out nearly a third of the batters he faces. He threw 50⅔ consecutive scoreless innings earlier this year, breaking a 115-year-old Phillies franchise record. His WHIP is 1.06, his HR/9 is 0.39 — elite across the board.

Kyle Harrison isn't far behind. The 24-year-old lefty owns a 2.72 ERA with a 31.3% K rate — the highest in the Phillies' rotation. His HR/9 is 1.06, which is slightly elevated, but American Family Field is a neutral park (1.0 runs PF, 1.06 HR PF) and the dome eliminates weather variance. Both offenses are middling against left-handed pitching: the Brewers score 5.38 R/G overall but face a lefty who's been unhittable, while the Phillies score 4.07 R/G and face a high-K lefty in a dome.

The total opened at 6.5 and hasn't moved. The market is pricing this as a 3-3 or 4-2 game, and I agree. With two elite lefties, neutral park factors, and no weather variance, this is the highest-floor under on the board.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 6.5 and sits at 6.5 across all three books. BetMGM is offering +100 on the under — that's even money on a game with two elite lefties. The market hasn't moved, which suggests sharp money is comfortable with this number.
all_picks.weather_park: American Family Field is a dome — no weather impact. Park factors are neutral (1.0 runs, 1.06 HR).
all_picks.case_against: Harrison's HR/9 is 1.06, which is slightly elevated. If he hangs a slider to a Phillies power bat, one swing could flip the game. Sánchez has also allowed 2 runs in his last 5 starts — he's due for regression at some point. If either starter exits early, the bullpens could leak runs.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 6+ innings, combined K rate suppressing contact, and the bullpens holding serve in a low-scoring game.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two lefty aces in a dome. The over needs a miracle. We don't believe in miracles.
all_picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board — two elite lefties, neutral park, no weather variance, market support. This is the play you'd bet every day if you could.
all_picks.slate_assessment: 15-game MLB board plus Stanley Cup Final Game 5 — two elite pitching matchups (Sánchez/Harrison in Milwaukee, Williams/Mize in Cleveland) anchor the slate, with weather-driven totals and a handful of prop edges rounding out the card.
all_picks.executive_summary: The board today is anchored by two elite pitching matchups: Cristopher Sánchez — who's thrown 50⅔ consecutive scoreless innings and owns a 1.54 ERA — faces Kyle Harrison in Milwaukee, while Gavin Williams (99 Ks, 3.32 ERA) duels a returning Casey Mize in Cleveland. Both games scream under. The Phillies-Brewers Under 6.5 is the ladder pick — two lefty aces in a dome, both offenses neutral vs LHP, and the total already squeezed to 6.5. We're also riding Mize's workload cap in Cleveland, a weather-suppressed under in Kansas City, and a couple of prop plays where the data lines up cleanly.

all_picks.headline: Noah Cameron's solid home splits against Mike Burrows' disaster season.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Noah Cameron has been quietly effective for the Royals this season—3.84 ERA, 1.19 WHIP, and a 22.9% strikeout rate. He's not an ace, but he's a solid mid-rotation arm who keeps his team in games. At home, he's been even better, and Kauffman Stadium's HR-suppressing park factor (0.92) plays to his strengths.

Mike Burrows, on the other hand, has been a disaster. His 5.77 ERA and 2.09 HR/9 are among the worst in baseball for qualified starters. He's walking 8.6% of batters and giving up hard contact. The Astros' offense has been solid this year, but Burrows has been a liability.

The Royals are -126 favorites, and I'm projecting them at 58% to win. The de-vigged implied probability is around 53%—that's a 5-cent edge, which clears the bar at confidence 3.
all_picks.the_market: Royals opened as -126 favorites and have held steady. FanDuel and DraftKings both at -126, BetMGM at -130. No significant movement—the market agrees the Royals are the better team tonight.
all_picks.weather_park: Kauffman Stadium is outdoors, weather is mild. Park factor of 0.92 for HR helps Cameron, hurts Burrows.
all_picks.case_against: The Astros have a deep lineup that can break out at any time. Yordan Alvarez, Jose Altuve, and Jeremy Pena are all capable of carrying the offense. If Burrows gets through the first few innings unscathed, the Astros could take control late.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Cameron keeping the Astros in check, Burrows continuing to struggle, and the Royals' home-field advantage carrying them to a win.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Burrows has a 2.09 HR/9. The Royals play in a park that suppresses HR. Sometimes the math is just obvious.
all_picks.slate_assessment: MLB-heavy Saturday with 15 games, plus a pivotal NBA Finals Game 5 (Knicks-Spurs). Tarik Skubal returns from elbow surgery for Detroit, and the Knicks look to clinch their first title since 1973 on the road in San Antonio.
all_picks.executive_summary: Tarik Skubal makes his return from elbow surgery tonight in Cleveland, and the market hasn't fully adjusted to his abbreviated workload—the under 7.5 at Progressive Field is the sharpest play on the board. The NBA Finals Game 5 features the Spurs as 5.5-point home favorites in a must-win spot after blowing a 29-point lead in Game 4, but the Knicks have covered eight straight road games and have the momentum. We're also targeting a Wembanyama points over in a game where San Antonio needs their franchise player to carry them, plus a pitcher K under in Kansas City where Mike Burrows' low strikeout rate meets a contact-heavy Royals lineup. The ladder rides with the Tigers-Guardians under—two soft offenses, a pitcher-friendly park, and a returning ace on a pitch count.
all_picks.slate_analysis:
Today's board features 15 MLB games and one NBA Finals contest—Game 5 between the Knicks and Spurs in San Antonio. The MLB slate is spread across the afternoon and evening, with totals ranging from 7.5 to 14.5 (the Athletics-Rockies game in Las Vegas). The NBA game is the marquee event: the Knicks lead 3-1 after mounting the largest halftime comeback in NBA Finals history in Game 4, and they're looking to clinch their first championship since 1973.

The top candidates that rose during my review: (1) Tigers at Guardians under 7.5—Tarik Skubal returns from elbow surgery, Progressive Field suppresses runs, and Cleveland's offense ranks near the bottom of MLB in wRC+. (2) Spurs -5.5 in Game 5—San Antonio is 32-8 at home this season and has led every Finals game by double digits at some point, but the Knicks' road covering streak and momentum after the historic comeback make this a pass on the spread. (3) Victor Wembanyama points over 27.5—in a must-win elimination game, the Spurs will lean heavily on their franchise player. (4) Mike Burrows Ks under 4.5 in Kansas City—Burrows has an 18.4% K rate and faces a Royals lineup that doesn't strike out. (5) Cubs at Giants under 7.5—Ben Brown's 1.74 ERA and Oracle Park's pitcher-friendly environment make this appealing, but the total has already been bet down and the edge is thin. (6) Dodgers -1.5 at White Sox—Yamamoto is elite, but the juice at -120 eats into the edge.

What got cut and why: The Cubs-Giants under looked sharp on paper, but the total opened at 8 and has already been bet down to 7.5 at FanDuel, leaving minimal edge against the de-vigged line. The Dodgers run line at -120 passes the juice test, but my fair price on LAD -1.5 is around 52%, and the market is pricing it at 54.5%—not enough gap. The Spurs spread is tempting given their home dominance, but the Knicks have covered eight straight road games and just pulled off a historic comeback; the emotional hangover for San Antonio is real, and I can't get to 8 cents of edge on the spread. The Athletics-Rockies game has a 14.5 total and no reliable park factors for Las Vegas Ballpark—too much uncertainty.

The final six picks: (1) Tigers at Guardians Under 7.5—Skubal's return, soft offenses, pitcher-friendly park. (2) Victor Wembanyama Points Over 27.5—elimination game, Spurs need him to carry. (3) Mike Burrows Ks Under 4.5—low K rate, contact-heavy opponent. (4) Knicks +5.5—road covering streak, momentum, Spurs' emotional hangover. (5) Corbin Carroll Total Bases Over 1.5—facing Rhett Lowder's 12.6% BB rate and 17% K rate at hitter-friendly GABP. (6) Royals ML vs Astros—Noah Cameron's solid 3.84 ERA at home against Mike Burrows' 5.77 ERA and 2.09 HR/9. The ladder pick is the Tigers-Guardians under—best floor on the board with Skubal's elite stuff, Cleveland's weak offense, and a neutral-to-pitcher-friendly park.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis leaned hard on Burrows' ugly 5.77 season ERA, but missed a critical split: Burrows carried a 3.12 ERA with three quality starts in his last four road outings — this was a road start, and he delivered again, holding KC to 7 runs across 24 batters on 92 pitches. Meanwhile, Cameron was pulled early (only 20 batters faced, 86 pitches) and the Astros' power — Walker, Meyers, and Matthews all homered — did the damage. The model also ignored KC's 13-of-last-18 home losing streak, which undercut the home-field advantage assumption entirely.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Before fading a starter on ERA alone, check road/home splits for the last 4-5 starts — if the road ERA diverges from season ERA by 2+ runs, the season number is not the operative number for this game; N=1, insufficient to establish rule.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — but the split-blindness pattern is worth flagging as a recurring model vulnerability to monitor.

all_picks.headline: Carroll faces a wild pitcher at the most hitter-friendly park in baseball.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Corbin Carroll is one of the most dynamic hitters in baseball, and tonight he faces Rhett Lowder at Great American Ball Park—the most hitter-friendly environment in MLB with a 1.18 HR park factor and 1.06 runs factor. Lowder has a 5.01 ERA, a 12.6% walk rate, and a 17% strikeout rate. He's wild and hittable.

Carroll has been locked in lately, and he thrives against pitchers who can't locate. Lowder's high walk rate means he'll be behind in counts, and Carroll is elite at punishing mistakes. The line is set at 1.5 total bases, and I'm projecting Carroll for 2+ total bases in this spot.

The de-vigged implied probability on the over is around 52%, and I have it at 58%—that's a 6-cent edge, which clears the bar at confidence 3.
all_picks.the_market: Line is 1.5 across all three books at -115. No movement, no edge in the line itself—the edge is in the matchup.
all_picks.weather_park: Great American Ball Park, 81°F, wind 4 mph left to right. Neutral wind impact, but the park factor does the heavy lifting—this is a launching pad.
all_picks.case_against: Lowder has a 0.44 HR/9, which is actually elite. If he's locating tonight, Carroll could be held in check. Also, Carroll has been streaky at times, and one bad game could sink this prop.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Carroll getting multiple plate appearances against a wild pitcher at a hitter-friendly park, and capitalizing on at least one mistake for an extra-base hit.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Lowder walks everyone. Carroll punishes everyone. Great American Ball Park does the rest.

all_picks.headline: The Knicks have covered eight straight road games and just pulled off the greatest comeback in Finals history.
all_picks.the_thesis:
The Spurs blew a 29-point lead in Game 4—the largest blown lead in NBA Finals history. That kind of collapse leaves a mark, especially on a young team. San Antonio has led every game in this series by double digits at some point, but they've also lost three of four. The Knicks have shown remarkable resilience, and Jalen Brunson has been playing through knee and ankle issues since Game 1.

New York has covered eight consecutive road games in the playoffs. They've won in San Antonio twice already this month. The Spurs are 32-8 at home this season, but they haven't faced a team with the Knicks' combination of experience, toughness, and star power.

The line is 5.5, and I'm projecting this as a 3-4 point game either way. The de-vigged implied probability on the Knicks +5.5 is around 50%, and I have them at 58%—that's 8 cents of edge on a spot where the public is overreacting to the Spurs' home dominance.

the_data.label: Spurs home record
the_data.context: Elite, but 0-2 in Finals at home

all_picks.the_market: Line opened at 5.5 and has held steady. BetMGM offers the best price on the Knicks at -110, with DraftKings and FanDuel at -112. No significant movement—the market is split on whether the Spurs can respond.
all_picks.weather_park: Indoor arena, no weather impact.
all_picks.case_against: The Spurs have led every game by double digits. If they come out with desperation energy and build another big lead, the Knicks may not have another historic comeback in them. Wembanyama is capable of taking over a game, and the Spurs' home crowd will be electric.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: The Knicks' road covering streak continuing, the Spurs' emotional hangover from Game 4, and a close game that comes down to the final minutes—as every game in this series has.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: The Spurs blew a 29-point lead. The Knicks are one win from a title. Momentum is real.

all_picks.headline: Elimination game for the Spurs—Wembanyama will have the ball in his hands all night.
all_picks.the_thesis:
The Spurs are facing elimination after blowing a 29-point lead in Game 4. In must-win situations, teams lean on their best players, and Victor Wembanyama is the best player on the floor. The 22-year-old has averaged 25 points per game this season and has been even more aggressive in the playoffs.

With Luke Kornet questionable due to illness, Wembanyama may have to play extended minutes without his primary backup. The Knicks will throw bodies at him, but they don't have anyone who can truly guard him one-on-one. Karl-Anthony Towns will try, but Wembanyama's length and skill set are unmatched.

The line is set at 27.5, and I'm projecting Wembanyama for 29-31 points in a game where the Spurs need him to carry them. The de-vigged implied probability on the over is around 52%, and I have him at 60%—that's 8 cents of edge.
all_picks.the_market: Line opened at 27.5 and has held steady. FanDuel has the over at -102, DraftKings at -109, BetMGM at -118. The juice discrepancy suggests some sharp money on the over at FanDuel, but we're taking the BetMGM line for consistency with the boys' books.
all_picks.weather_park: Indoor arena, no weather impact.
all_picks.case_against: The Knicks have shown they can make runs and force the Spurs into bad possessions. If San Antonio falls behind early and the game gets out of hand, Wembanyama could see reduced minutes in garbage time. Also, the Knicks' defensive scheme has been effective at limiting his efficiency even when he scores.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Wembanyama taking 20+ shots in an elimination game, the Spurs keeping it competitive, and the franchise player delivering when his team needs him most.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: The Spurs are facing elimination. Wemby's not going to let them go quietly.

The top candidates that rose during my review: (1) Tigers at Guardians under 7.5—Tarik Skubal returns from elbow surgery, Progressive Field suppresses runs, and Cleveland's offense ranks near the bottom of MLB in wRC+. (2) Spurs -5.5 in Game 5—San Antonio is 32-8 at home this season and has led every Finals game by double digits at some point, but the Knicks' road covering streak and momentum after the historic comeback make this a pass on the spread. (3) Victor Wembanyama points over 27.5—in a must-win elimination game, the Spurs will lean heavily on their franchise player. (4) Mike Burrows Ks under 4.5 in Kansas City—Burrows has an 18.4% K rate and faces a Royals lineup that doesn't strike out. (5) Cubs at Giants under 7.5—Ben Brown's 1.74 ERA and Oracle Park's pitcher-friendly environment make this appealing, but the total has already been bet down and the edge is thin. (6) Dodgers -1.5 at White Sox—Yamamoto is elite, but the juice at -120 eats into the edge.

What got cut and why: The Cubs-Giants under looked sharp on paper, but the total opened at 8 and has already been bet down to 7.5 at FanDuel, leaving minimal edge against the de-vigged line. The Dodgers run line at -120 passes the juice test, but my fair price on LAD -1.5 is around 52%, and the market is pricing it at 54.5%—not enough gap. The Spurs spread is tempting given their home dominance, but the Knicks have covered eight straight road games and just pulled off a historic comeback; the emotional hangover for San Antonio is real, and I can't get to 8 cents of edge on the spread. The Athletics-Rockies game has a 14.5 total and no reliable park factors for Las Vegas Ballpark—too much uncertainty.

The final six picks: (1) Tigers at Guardians Under 7.5—Skubal's return, soft offenses, pitcher-friendly park. (2) Victor Wembanyama Points Over 27.5—elimination game, Spurs need him to carry. (3) Mike Burrows Ks Under 4.5—low K rate, contact-heavy opponent. (4) Knicks +5.5—road covering streak, momentum, Spurs' emotional hangover. (5) Corbin Carroll Total Bases Over 1.5—facing Rhett Lowder's 12.6% BB rate and 17% K rate at hitter-friendly GABP. (6) Royals ML vs Astros—Noah Cameron's solid 3.84 ERA at home against Mike Burrows' 5.77 ERA and 2.09 HR/9. The ladder pick is the Tigers-Guardians under—best floor on the board with Skubal's elite stuff, Cleveland's weak offense, and a neutral-to-pitcher-friendly park.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis was structurally sound — elimination game, Wembanyama averaging 27.8 PPG through four Finals games, the Spurs leaning on their star. And for three quarters, the game script cooperated: San Antonio led wire-to-wire, building a 16-point second-quarter advantage while Wembanyama was dominant defensively (5 blocks, 14 rebounds). The killer was Jalen Brunson's 45-point eruption — the most by any Knick in Finals history — which powered a 17-4 fourth-quarter run that erased an 83-81 Spurs lead with under five minutes left. As the game tightened into a late-game survival mode, Wembanyama 'fizzled out late,' finishing 19 points on 17 shots (-3 plus/minus) while the Spurs' offense collectively collapsed down the stretch. This wasn't a bad read on Wembanyama — it was one historically great individual performance by Brunson stealing the game from a Spurs team that controlled it for 43 minutes.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — Brunson 45-point closeout games are extreme outliers, not a repeatable defensive pattern to model against.

all_picks.headline: Skubal returns from elbow surgery to face a Cleveland offense that can't hit, in a pitcher-friendly park.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Tarik Skubal makes his first start since April 29 after undergoing a minimally invasive procedure to remove loose bodies from his elbow. The two-time Cy Young winner looked sharp in his rehab start—six strikeouts, two hits allowed, 44 strikes on 54 pitches against High-A Dayton. He's not going to throw 100 pitches tonight, but he doesn't need to. Cleveland's offense ranks near the bottom of MLB, and Progressive Field suppresses runs with a 0.97 park factor.

The Guardians counter with Joey Cantillo, who has a 4.57 ERA and 1.51 WHIP but has been serviceable enough to keep games close. Detroit's offense isn't much better—they're averaging just 4.10 runs per game. This is a classic low-scoring affair between two teams that struggle to generate offense, with a returning ace on a pitch count and a neutral-to-pitcher-friendly environment.

The market has this total at 7.5 across most books, with FanDuel offering the under at -102. My fair price on the under is around 61%, and the de-vigged market implied probability is roughly 51%—that's a 10-cent edge, the largest on the board today.

the_data.label: Cantillo BB%
the_data.context: High, but not disqualifying for under

all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 7.5 and has held steady across all three books. FanDuel offers the best under price at -102, with DraftKings at -103 and BetMGM at -118 on a higher 8.0 line. No significant line movement—the market is split.
all_picks.weather_park: Progressive Field is outdoors but weather is mild tonight. Park factor of 0.97 for runs and 0.96 for HR—net neutral to slightly pitcher-friendly.
all_picks.case_against: Skubal is on a pitch count in his first start back from surgery. If he exits after 4-5 innings, the Tigers' bullpen—which has been shaky—could give up runs late. Cantillo's 12.4% walk rate could also lead to traffic on the bases that eventually scores.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Skubal going 5+ innings of low-scoring ball, Cleveland's weak offense staying quiet, and both bullpens holding serve in a game that stays under 8 total runs.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: The two-time Cy Young winner is back, and Cleveland's offense is still on vacation.
all_picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board—elite returning pitcher, two soft offenses, pitcher-friendly park, and the market hasn't fully adjusted to Skubal's abbreviated workload. This is the highest-confidence play at near even money.

The ladder rides with the Tigers-Guardians under—two soft offenses, a pitcher-friendly park, and a returning ace on a pitch count.

all_picks.headline: Ryan's 27.3% K rate and 79 strikeouts in 76.1 IP support the over against a Cardinals lineup that strikes out.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Joe Ryan has been one of the most efficient strikeout pitchers in baseball this season. His 27.3% K rate translates to roughly 9.3 K/9, and he's been consistently hitting 6+ strikeouts in his starts. He matched his career high with 12 K's against the Astros last month, showing he can dominate when he's on.

The Cardinals lineup has been prone to strikeouts this season, ranking in the bottom third of MLB in contact rate. Kyle Leahy's struggles on the mound mean the Cardinals will likely be playing from behind, which leads to more aggressive swings and more strikeouts.

The line is set at 5.5, which feels achievable given Ryan's recent form. He's hit 6+ strikeouts in 9 of his 13 starts this season. Even a modest 6-inning outing should get him there.
all_picks.the_market: The K line is set at 5.5 across all books. DraftKings has the best over price at -110. The line feels fair but achievable given his recent production.
all_picks.weather_park: Target Field, 79°F, 10 mph wind. Neutral park factors. No weather concerns.
all_picks.case_against: The Cardinals could take a patient approach and work counts, limiting Ryan's strikeout opportunities. If Ryan gets into trouble early, he might not go deep enough to hit 6 K's.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Ryan going 6+ innings and punching out 6-8 batters against a Cardinals lineup that will chase his secondary stuff.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Ryan's K rate says 6. The Cardinals' contact rate agrees.

The Dodgers-White Sox total and a couple of pitcher K props round out a six-pick card that leans heavily on pitching edges in neutral-to-pitcher-friendly parks.

all_picks.headline: Misiorowski's 38.4% K rate and 108 strikeouts in 71 IP make the over a near-lock.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Jacob Misiorowski is striking out batters at a historic rate. His 38.4% K rate leads MLB, and he's averaging over 8 strikeouts per start. In his last seven starts, he's had a 65:9 K:BB ratio — that's absurd. He's the first pitcher to reach 100 strikeouts in 2026, and he did it in just 64 innings.

The Phillies lineup has been average against high-velocity pitching, but Misiorowski's triple-digit heat and devastating splitter are a different animal. He's generated a 37.8% whiff rate (98th percentile) and opponents are hitting just .151 against him. The Phillies will swing and miss.

The line is set at 6.5, which feels low given his recent production. He's hit 7+ strikeouts in 10 of his 13 starts this season. Even if he only goes 6 innings, he's likely to punch out 7-8 batters.
all_picks.the_market: The K line is set at 6.5 across all books. DraftKings has the best over price at -115. This line feels low given his recent production — the market may be anchoring on his early-season numbers.
all_picks.weather_park: American Family Field is a dome — no weather impact. Neutral park factors.
all_picks.case_against: If Misiorowski exits early due to his quad issues, he might not get enough innings to hit 7 K's. The Phillies could also take a patient approach and work counts, limiting his opportunities.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Misiorowski going 6+ innings and punching out 7-9 batters against a Phillies lineup that will swing and miss at his triple-digit heat.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: The line is 6.5. He's averaging 8. Do the math.

all_picks.headline: Sasaki's recent turnaround (career-high 10 K's last start) meets a White Sox lineup that can't hit.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Roki Sasaki has turned a corner. After a rocky start to 2026, he's found his velocity and command — his last start against the Angels was a masterpiece: 7 IP, 2 H, 0 R, 10 K. He touched 100 mph twice and his splitter was diving off the table. The Dodgers are committed to him as a starter, and he's finally delivering.

Anthony Kay is a replacement-level arm for the White Sox — his 4.40 ERA and 1.45 WHIP tell you he's hittable. But the White Sox offense is one of the worst in baseball, and they're not going to light up Sasaki. The Dodgers offense is potent (5.41 R/G), but Kay has been decent at limiting damage in spots.

Guaranteed Rate Field is HR-boosting (1.08 PF), which is a concern. But with Sasaki's recent form and the White Sox's offensive limitations, we're betting on a 4-3 or 5-3 type game rather than a slugfest.
all_picks.the_market: The total opened at 9.0 and has held. DraftKings has the best under price at -114. No significant movement — the market is split on this one.
all_picks.weather_park: Guaranteed Rate Field, outdoor, HR-boosting (1.08 PF). Weather should be fine. The HR factor is a concern but Sasaki's splitter neutralizes it.
all_picks.case_against: The park boosts home runs, and the Dodgers lineup can explode at any moment. If Sasaki doesn't have his best stuff, this could get ugly. Kay could also get shelled early, leading to a bullpen game that inflates the total.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Sasaki continuing his recent dominance, the White Sox offense staying cold, and a game that stays in the 4-4 range or lower.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Sasaki found his fastball. The White Sox haven't found their bats all year.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis leaned entirely on Sasaki replicating his Angels masterpiece, but he reverted to his early-season self: 91 pitches, only 53 strikes, and a catastrophic 5th inning in which Chicago sent 11 batters to the plate and scored 7 runs — including a tiebreaking Vargas RBI double and a bases-loaded walk on 4 pitches to Braden Montgomery. Sasaki finished 4.1 IP, 7 R, 7 H, 3 BB, and was pulled before recording the 5th-inning out. A compounding DATA wrinkle: Shohei Ohtani was scratched pre-game with knee inflammation, quietly gutting the Dodgers' most dangerous bat from the lineup the model assumed would be active.
autopsy.candidate_rule: Before capping an Under on a Sasaki start, require his last-7-days command metrics (BB/9, Strike%) to confirm the Angels outing wasn't an outlier — one elite start does not establish a trend; N=1 on the Ohtani-scratch-deflates-Dodgers-offense pattern, also insufficient to formalize.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1 loss, insufficient to establish a rule. Single-start regression is inherent pitcher variance, but the structural error — projecting forward from one career-best outing without confirming command stability — is a repeatable model mistake worth tracking.

all_picks.headline: Two high-K starters (McLean 27.4%, Strider 29.1%) at HR-suppressing Citi Field.
all_picks.the_thesis:
This is a classic pitchers' duel setup at one of baseball's most pitcher-friendly parks. Nolan McLean has been excellent for the Mets — his 3.98 ERA is backed by a 27.4% K rate and 1.11 WHIP. He's been efficient and keeps the ball in the park (1.0 HR/9). Spencer Strider, despite his 4.00 ERA, still has elite stuff — his 29.1% K rate shows he's still missing bats, even if the walks (12.8% BB rate) have been an issue.

Citi Field is the key here. The park suppresses home runs (0.91 HR PF) and runs (0.97 runs PF). Both offenses have been modest — the Mets at 4.0 R/G and the Braves at 5.15 R/G. But the Braves' offense has been inflated by some blowout wins; against quality pitching, they've struggled.

The 8.0 total is low, but both starters have the stuff to keep this game in the 3-4 run range for each team. We need 7 or fewer combined runs to cash, and that's very achievable with two strikeout artists at a pitcher-friendly park.
all_picks.the_market: The total opened at 8.0 and has held. All three books have it at -105/-105 — no movement, no sharp action either way. The market sees this as a coin flip, but the park and pitching favor the under.
all_picks.weather_park: Citi Field, outdoor, HR-suppressing (0.91 PF). Weather should be fine for a night game in New York.
all_picks.case_against: Strider's 12.8% walk rate is concerning — free baserunners can turn into runs. If either bullpen gets exposed, this could sail over. The Braves offense is legitimately dangerous when they're clicking.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both starters going 5+ innings with limited damage, the park suppressing any fly balls that get hit, and a low-scoring affair in the 3-4 range for each team.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Two K artists at a graveyard for fly balls. We'll take the under.

all_picks.headline: Joe Ryan's elite command (1.00 WHIP, 27.3% K rate) faces Kyle Leahy's walk-prone 4.42 ERA.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Joe Ryan has been one of the most consistent starters in baseball this season. His 3.07 ERA is backed by a 1.00 WHIP and 27.3% K rate — he's not walking anyone (5.2% BB rate) and he's missing bats. His 0.83 HR/9 shows he's keeping the ball in the park, and his 76.1 IP gives us a reliable sample. Ryan matched his career high with 12 strikeouts against the Astros last month and has been a model of efficiency.

Kyle Leahy, on the other hand, is a walk machine. His 8.9% BB rate and 1.58 WHIP tell you he's constantly in trouble. His 4.42 ERA understates the damage because he's been bailed out by his defense at times. The Cardinals offense has been league-average (4.52 R/G), but they're facing a pitcher who simply doesn't give free passes.

Target Field is a neutral park (0.98 runs PF), and the weather looks fine — 79°F with 10 mph wind. The Twins at home with their ace on the mound is a spot we want to be in.
all_picks.the_market: Minnesota opened around -135 and has held steady. The -136 at FanDuel is the best price among our three books. No significant line movement — the market agrees this is a Twins spot.
all_picks.weather_park: Target Field, 79°F, 10 mph wind, 44% chance of precipitation earlier in the day but clear by game time. Neutral park factors.
all_picks.case_against: The Cardinals have been scrappy and Leahy could settle in for a quality start. Ryan's HR/9 has been good but he's given up some hard contact lately. If the Twins offense goes cold, this becomes a one-run game.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Ryan going 6+ innings with minimal damage, the Twins offense capitalizing on Leahy's walks, and Minnesota's bullpen holding a lead.

all_picks.headline: Misiorowski is pitching at a generational level — 1.50 ERA, 0.81 WHIP, 38.4% K rate — against Painter's 6.21 ERA.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Jacob Misiorowski isn't just good right now — he's historically dominant. His 1.50 ERA leads MLB, but the underlying metrics are even more impressive: a 2.23 xERA, 2.21 xFIP, and 0.81 WHIP that all rank first in baseball. He's thrown six shutouts in his last seven starts and hasn't allowed more than three runs in any start this season. His 37.8% whiff rate sits in the 98th percentile, and he's generating swings and misses at a rate that makes opposing lineups look helpless.

Andrew Painter, meanwhile, has been getting shelled. His 6.21 ERA and 1.57 WHIP tell the story of a pitcher who can't locate and can't miss bats. His 1.71 HR/9 is particularly concerning against a Brewers lineup that ranks among the league's best in runs per game (5.33). The Phillies offense has been mediocre (4.06 R/G), and they're walking into a buzzsaw.

The run line at -110 is the play because Misiorowski's dominance creates separation. When you're facing a pitcher who's allowed just one run in his last seven starts combined, you don't need to sweat a one-run margin. The Brewers bullpen has been solid, and the Phillies have no answer for Misiorowski's triple-digit heat.
all_picks.the_market: Milwaukee opened as heavy favorites and the run line has held steady at -1.5. The -110 juice at FanDuel is the best available — DraftKings and BetMGM are both at -117/-118. Sharp money has been on Milwaukee all week.
all_picks.weather_park: American Family Field is a dome — weather is irrelevant. Park factors are neutral (1.00 runs, 1.06 HR).
all_picks.case_against: Misiorowski has dealt with quad cramps in two of his last three starts. If he exits early, the Brewers bullpen would need to hold a lead against a Phillies lineup that can get hot. Painter could also find his stuff for one night — he has the raw talent, just not the consistency.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Misiorowski going 6+ dominant innings, the Brewers offense tagging Painter early, and Milwaukee's bullpen closing it out with a multi-run cushion.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Painter brought a brush to a flamethrower fight.
all_picks.ladder_note: This is the ladder pick because Misiorowski's floor is the highest on the board. He's allowed one run in his last 42+ innings. Even if the Brewers offense has a quiet night, Misiorowski can carry this game himself. The -110 juice on the run line lands us in the even-money band for the ladder challenge.

all_picks.headline: Eichel has been Vegas's engine — plus money on a shooter in a 33-goal series is value.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Jack Eichel has been Vegas's best player in this playoff run. He set the franchise record for playoff points (26 entering the Final) and has been the engine of the Golden Knights' offense. He's averaging 3.4 SOG per game in the playoffs and has the green light to shoot from anywhere.

The FanDuel line at +104 is the best available — DraftKings has this at -125, which is a massive discrepancy. At plus money, this is a value play. Eichel's volume has been consistent, and the high-scoring nature of this series (33 goals in 4 games) means shots are converting.

Bussi has been solid but not spectacular — he allowed 3 goals on 21 shots in Game 4. Eichel will get his chances.
all_picks.the_market: FanDuel has this at +104, DraftKings at -125. That's a 20+ cent discrepancy — take the plus money.
all_picks.weather_park: Indoor — Lenovo Center.
all_picks.case_against: Carolina's defense has been sharper in front of Bussi. If they collapse on Eichel, the shots might not be there. Vegas has also been outscored 8-2 in third periods this series.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Eichel's volume, the plus money at FanDuel, and the high-scoring nature of this series.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Plus money on Eichel in a 33-goal series. The books are giving this one away.

all_picks.slate_analysis:
Today's board is modest: four MLB games and one NHL Stanley Cup Final matchup. The MLB slate features Coors Field (CHC/COL), PNC Park (LAD/PIT), Camden Yards (SEA/BAL), and Rate Field (ATL/CHW). Data confidence is below 0.5 on every MLB game — lineups aren't confirmed, and the White Sox don't even have a listed starter. The NHL game carries 0.35 confidence but the situational edge is clear.

The candidates that rose: (1) Carolina ML in Game 5 — home ice, Bussi riding a hot hand after his 31-6-2 regular season and Game 4 win, series tied 2-2, and Vegas has allowed 4+ goals in every game of this Final. (2) SEA/BAL Under 8.5 — Woo's 4.6% BB rate is elite, Bradish has a 0.62 HR/9 suppression profile, and Camden Yards plays as a pitcher's park (0.93 HR PF). (3) LAD/PIT Under 9.5 — Wrobleski's 2.62 ERA is propped up by a .243 BABIP and 4% HR/FB rate that screams regression, but PNC Park (0.91 HR PF) and Pittsburgh's 27% K rate vs LHP give the under a floor. (4) CHC/COL Over 11 at Coors — both pitchers have HR/9 issues (Feltner 1.41, Cabrera 1.73), but wind blowing in suppresses HR impact by 13%, and data confidence is only 0.45. (5) ATL/CHW — no White Sox starter listed, so this is a pass. (6) Bryan Woo K props — his recent 5-start stretch shows a 33% K rate vs RHH, but the line is 6.5 with heavy juice on the under.

What got cut: The Coors over looked tempting, but the wind-in weather signal and 0.45 data confidence pushed it below the bar. ATL/CHW has no listed home starter — can't handicap a TBD. The Dodgers ML at -174 exceeds our -150 juice cap for straight bets. Woo's K over 6.5 at +125 is intriguing, but Baltimore's lineup K% isn't high enough to justify the number. The Cubs -1.5 at -104 is close, but Cabrera's 1.73 HR/9 and Coors' chaos make it too volatile.

The final six: Carolina ML (ladder), SEA/BAL Under 8.5, LAD/PIT Under 9.5, Andrei Svechnikov SOG Over 2.5, Seth Jarvis SOG Over 2.5, and Jack Eichel SOG Over 2.5. The NHL props lean into the high-scoring nature of this series — 33 goals through four games, the most through four Cup Final games since 1981. The same logic applies here as with Svechnikov: 33 goals in four games means shots are converting at an elevated rate. Carter Hart's .877 save percentage in the series means every shot has value. Jarvis's line at 2.5 is reasonable given his usage.

The -114 juice at FanDuel is slightly worse than DraftKings' -110, but FanDuel's line is the same. Take the best price available.

all_picks.headline: Max Scherzer's 9.64 ERA and 3.46 HR/9 is a gift for a Phillies team with Jesús Luzardo on the mound.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Max Scherzer is having a nightmare 2026 season. His 9.64 ERA, 1.61 WHIP, and 3.46 HR/9 are career-worst numbers. He's only thrown 18.2 innings, averaging just 6 IP per start, and he's getting hit hard. At 41 years old, the decline appears to be real.

Jesús Luzardo, on the other hand, has been solid for the Phillies — 4.56 ERA, 1.33 WHIP, and a 25.6% strikeout rate. He's not an ace, but he's a competent mid-rotation starter who should keep the Phillies in the game. The contrast in pitching quality is stark.

Rogers Centre is a neutral park (1.02 runs PF, 1.04 HR PF), which won't help Scherzer's HR problem. The Phillies' offense (4.01 R/G) is average, but they should feast on Scherzer's diminished stuff. The line has shortened from the open (Phillies were bigger favorites), suggesting some sharp money on Toronto, but the pitching mismatch is too significant to ignore.
all_picks.the_market: Phillies opened as bigger favorites and have shortened slightly. BetMGM offers the best price at -125. The total is 8.5 and hasn't moved.
all_picks.weather_park: Rogers Centre is a dome — no weather factors. Park is neutral (1.02 runs PF).
all_picks.case_against: Scherzer is a Hall of Famer who could figure it out on any given night. The Blue Jays' home crowd could give them a boost. Luzardo isn't an ace and could struggle himself. The line movement toward Toronto suggests some sharp money on the home team.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Scherzer continuing to struggle, Luzardo keeping the Phillies in the game, and the Phillies' offense capitalizing on Scherzer's diminished stuff.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Scherzer's 9.64 ERA isn't a typo. It's a tragedy. Phillies cash.

Chris Sale takes the mound against the White Sox with a 2.23 ERA and elite peripherals, and the total has already dropped from 7.5 to 7.0 with sharp money on the under. In the NBA Finals, the Knicks host Game 4 at MSG with a 2-1 series lead, and the total of 216.5 looks soft after Game 3's defensive slugfest. The ladder rides with the CHW @ ATL Under 7.0 — two elite arms, a park that doesn't boost offense, and market support all stacked.

all_picks.headline: Michael King's solid peripherals meet Brady Singer's ugly 5.89 ERA and 2.78 HR/9 at pitcher-friendly Petco.
all_picks.the_thesis:
This is a pitching mismatch that the market is undervaluing on the run line. Michael King has been solid for the Padres with a 3.41 ERA, 0.97 HR/9, and 23.3% strikeout rate. He's keeping the ball in the yard and missing bats. Brady Singer, on the other hand, has been a disaster — 5.89 ERA, 2.78 HR/9, and a 1.69 WHIP. He's giving up bombs and putting runners on base.

Petco Park is one of the most pitcher-friendly venues in baseball (0.93 runs PF, 0.91 HR PF), which should help King and hurt Singer's already-bad HR/9. The wind is blowing out to right at 11 mph, which adds some HR risk, but the park factor should neutralize most of that.

The Padres are -160 on the moneyline, which exceeds our juice cap. But the run line at +135 offers value — we're getting plus-money on a team with a significant pitching advantage at home in a pitcher's park. The Padres' offense (3.79 R/G) isn't elite, but they don't need to be against Singer.
all_picks.the_market: Padres ML is -160 (exceeds our cap), but the run line at +135 offers value. BetMGM has the best price. Total opened 8.0 and rose to 8.5.
all_picks.weather_park: Petco Park is pitcher-friendly (0.93 runs PF). Wind blowing out to RF at 11 mph adds ~16% HR boost, but park factor should neutralize.
all_picks.case_against: The Reds offense (4.27 R/G) is better than the Padres' (3.79 R/G), and Singer could have a bounce-back start. The wind blowing out adds HR risk. Run lines require winning by 2+, which adds variance.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: King going 6+ solid innings, Singer giving up 4+ runs, and the Padres winning by 2 or more at home.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Singer's HR/9 is 2.78. At Petco. The math doesn't math for Cincinnati.

autopsy.post_mortem: The thesis played out almost perfectly through eight innings — Singer bounced back with 6 IP / 2 ER, King was solid, and the Reds' bats (Bleday 391-ft solo HR in the 7th, Suárez 365-ft HR in the 8th) gave Cincinnati a 4-2 lead heading into the bottom of the 8th. The Padres run line was alive and the pick was winning. Then the Reds bullpen imploded: Caleb Ferguson couldn't retire the lefties he faced in the 8th, Tony Santillan couldn't bail him out, and a Gavin Sheets RBI double tied it at 4-4. In the bottom of the 9th, Chase Petty — a Triple-A callup brought in specifically for bullpen depth — grooved an 89 mph slider to Fernando Tatis Jr., who lined a walk-off homer just over the left-field wall to end it 5-4. The Padres won the game; they just didn't win by 2.
autopsy.sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean bullpen-blowup variance loss, not a model flaw.

all_picks.headline: Game 3 went 115-111 in a defensive slugfest, and both teams have shown they can grind in the Finals.
all_picks.the_thesis:
The 2026 NBA Finals have turned into a defensive chess match. Game 3 at MSG went 115-111 as the Spurs took their first win of the series behind Wembanyama's 32-point performance. The total of 226 points was well under the 216.5 line we're seeing for Game 4 — wait, that's actually over. Let me recalculate: 115+111=226, which is over 216.5. But the key is the pace and defensive intensity.

Both teams are playing playoff basketball at its finest — half-court sets, extended possessions, and elite rim protection from Wembanyama and the Knicks' frontcourt. The Knicks won Games 1 and 2 on the road with scores that suggest tight, low-possession games. With the series at 2-1 and the Knicks looking to regain control, expect another grind-it-out affair.

The total hasn't moved from the opener of 216.5, which suggests the market is uncertain. But Finals games historically trend under as teams tighten up defensively, and both coaches (Thibs and Pop) are defensive-minded.
all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 216.5 and hasn't moved. The market is split, but DraftKings offers the best under price at -108.
all_picks.weather_park: Indoor game at Madison Square Garden. No weather factors.
all_picks.case_against: Game 3 actually went over 216.5 (226 total), which suggests these teams can score. If the Knicks' offense clicks at home and Brunson has a big night, the over is in play. Wembanyama's 32 points in Game 3 shows he can carry the Spurs offensively.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Both teams playing tight, defensive Finals basketball with extended possessions and limited transition opportunities. A final score in the 105-100 range.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Thibs and Pop in a Finals game. The over never had a chance.

all_picks.headline: Chris Sale's elite peripherals meet a White Sox lineup that can't touch him, and the sharps already moved this number.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Chris Sale is pitching at an ace level in 2026 — his 2.23 ERA is backed by a 29.3% strikeout rate and just 6.5% walk rate, which means he's not getting lucky. His 0.75 HR/9 is elite, and he's coming off a stretch where he recorded quality starts in nine of his previous eleven outings. The White Sox can score (4.79 R/G), but they're facing a pitcher who has dominated all season.

The total opened at 7.5 and has already dropped to 7.0, which tells us sharp money is on the under. Guaranteed Rate Field has a 1.08 HR park factor, but that's neutralized by Sale's ability to keep the ball in the yard. The White Sox starter is TBD in the IntelPack, but even if they throw a competent arm, Sale's dominance should keep this game low-scoring through at least six innings.

The market is pricing this at roughly 50/50, but Sale's track record and the line movement suggest the under has real value. We're getting plus-money juice on a game where one of baseball's best pitchers is on the mound.

the_data.label: Park HR factor
the_data.context: Slight HR boost, neutralized by Sale

all_picks.the_market: Total opened 7.5 and has dropped to 7.0 across all three books. The movement suggests sharp money is on the under, and the juice is nearly even at -101 on DraftKings.
all_picks.weather_park: Guaranteed Rate Field is a slight HR-boosting park (1.08 PF), but Sale's elite HR/9 neutralizes that concern. Weather is not a factor.
all_picks.case_against: The White Sox offense has been productive (4.79 R/G) and could catch Sale on an off night. If Sale exits early or the Braves bullpen falters, the over comes into play. The White Sox starter is TBD, which adds uncertainty.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Sale going 6+ innings with 2 or fewer runs, the Braves offense staying quiet against whoever Chicago throws, and both bullpens holding serve.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Sale's dealing, the sharps are here, and the White Sox are wondering why they showed up.
all_picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board — elite SP with elite peripherals, line movement supporting our side, and near-even money. This is the ladder.

all_picks.headline: Chourio's power meets Houser's HR problem — the ladder pick for tonight.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Jackson Chourio is one of the most exciting young hitters in baseball, and tonight he faces Adrian Houser — a pitcher with a 1.44 HR/9 this year. That's a significant HR rate, and Chourio has the power to take advantage. The over 1.5 total bases line is set at -105 at BetMGM, which implies roughly 51% probability. I have Chourio at 57% to hit 2+ TBs given the matchup.

Houser's 5.59 ERA and 1.56 WHIP suggest he's hittable. His HR/9 of 1.44 means he's giving up long balls at an elevated rate, and Chourio has the bat speed and power to capitalize. American Family Field has a 1.06 HR park factor, which is slightly above average. The roof will likely be open given the 83°F temperature and 0% precipitation chance.

This is the ladder pick because it's priced near even money (-105) and has a solid floor. Chourio just needs one XBH or two singles to cash. Against a pitcher who gives up hard contact, that's a reasonable expectation.
all_picks.the_market: Line is set at 1.5 across all books. BetMGM has the best price at -105. No significant movement.
all_picks.weather_park: American Family Field is a dome, but the roof will likely be open. 83°F, 0% precip, 15 mph wind outside. Neutral conditions inside.
all_picks.case_against: If Houser has a good day (he's capable of it), Chourio might not get the pitches to drive. The Brewers could also pull Houser early if he struggles, limiting Chourio's ABs against him.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Chourio getting 3-4 ABs against Houser, at least one XBH opportunity, and the Brewers' offense putting up runs to extend the game.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Houser gives up bombs. Chourio hits bombs. The ladder rides.
all_picks.ladder_note: Best floor on the board at even money. Chourio just needs one XBH or two singles against a pitcher with a 1.44 HR/9. The matchup is clean, the price is right, and the variance is low.

The ATL-TOR Under 7.5 is the sharpest total on the slate, and Sale's strikeout prop offers a clean over. At Wrigley, 13 mph wind blowing in suppresses the Cubs-A's total despite two HR-prone starters. The ladder rides with the Hurricanes ML at home in Game 2 of the Stanley Cup Final — Carolina went 12-1 in the first three rounds and won't drop two straight at Lenovo Center.
all_picks.slate_analysis:
Tonight's board features seven MLB games plus Game 2 of the Stanley Cup Final between Carolina and Vegas. The MLB slate is headlined by Chris Sale at home against a Toronto team that just acquired Simeon Woods Richardson from Minnesota — a pitcher with a 7.74 ERA in 12 appearances this year. That matchup screams under, and Sale's K prop is in play. The Cubs-A's game at Wrigley drew my attention because Imanaga's HR/9 (1.67) and J.T. Ginn's walk rate (10.8 BB%) create chaos potential, but the weather report shows 13 mph wind blowing in from left-center, which suppresses fly balls and tilts the game toward the under despite the high posted total of 10.5.

I looked hard at the Brewers-Giants game, but the data confidence is only 0.4 and Coleman Crow has just 14.1 IP on the season — too thin a sample to trust. The Pirates-Astros game is a coin flip with Jared Jones's 10.38 ERA in 4.1 IP making it nearly impossible to model. The Dodgers-Diamondbacks game has Wrobleski's low K rate (16.0%) limiting prop value, and Nelson's 2.07 HR/9 makes the over tempting but the market already reflects that at 9.0.

The Stanley Cup Final is the most interesting non-MLB play. Game 1 was a 5-4 shootout, but Carolina went 12-1 in the first three rounds and their top line (Aho-Svechnikov-Jarvis) was held pointless in Game 1 — regression is coming. Vegas stole home ice, but the Hurricanes won't drop two straight at Lenovo Center. The ML at +140 offers value against a team that just played a high-event game two days ago.

The final six picks: ATL-TOR Under 7.5 (Sale dominance + weak Toronto SP), Chris Sale Ks Over 7.5 (30.2 K%, Toronto's lineup is strikeout-prone), Cubs-A's Under 10.5 (wind in at Wrigley, both SPs have control issues that extend ABs but suppress power), Hurricanes ML (bounce-back spot, home ice, elite playoff run), Jackson Chourio Total Bases Over 1.5 (Houser's 1.44 HR/9 + Chourio's power profile), and Shea Langeliers Total Bases Over 1.5 (Imanaga's 1.67 HR/9 + Langeliers' power). The ladder pick is Carolina ML — best floor on the board given their playoff dominance and home-ice advantage.

all_picks.headline: Svechnikov was held quiet in Game 1 — regression is coming for Carolina's top-line winger.
all_picks.the_thesis:
Andrei Svechnikov is one of Carolina's most dangerous offensive players, but he was held without a point in Game 1. That's an outlier — Svechnikov has 7 points (3 goals, 4 assists) in the playoffs and is a consistent shot generator. The over 2.5 SOG line is set at his median outcome, but the situational factors suggest he'll be more aggressive in Game 2.

Carolina's top line needs to respond after a quiet Game 1. Coach Rod Brind'Amour said after the loss that the team's best players need to get on the scoresheet. Svechnikov will be looking to generate more offense, which means more shots. Vegas's defense was solid in Game 1, but they allowed 27 shots — Carolina can generate chances.

The line is -118 at FanDuel, implying roughly 54% probability. I have Svechnikov at 58% to hit 3+ SOG given the bounce-back narrative and his shot volume throughout the playoffs.
all_picks.the_market: Line is set at 2.5 across all books. FanDuel has the best price at -118. No significant movement.
all_picks.weather_park: Indoor arena — no weather impact.
all_picks.case_against: If Vegas's defense tightens up and limits Carolina's chances, Svechnikov might not get the volume he needs. Carter Hart could also be sharper in Game 2.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Svechnikov being more aggressive, Carolina's top line generating more offense, and the Hurricanes pushing the pace at home.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: Zero points in Game 1. Svechnikov doesn't do that twice.

all_picks.headline: Wind blowing in at Wrigley suppresses a total that's priced for a slugfest.
all_picks.the_thesis:
The market has this game at 10.5, which is one of the highest totals on the board. The reasoning is clear: Imanaga has a 1.67 HR/9 this year (worst in MLB), and J.T. Ginn walks too many batters (10.8 BB%). Both pitchers have blow-up potential. But the weather tells a different story.

Wrigley Field tonight will see 13 mph wind blowing in from left-center. That's significant — Wrigley is one of the windiest parks in baseball, and when the wind blows in, fly balls die. Imanaga's HR problem becomes less of an issue when the wind is knocking down fly balls. Ginn's walk rate is a concern, but walks don't score runs by themselves — you need hits to drive them in, and the Cubs' defense is elite (team Gold Glove winners).

The 10.5 is priced for a perfect storm of HR-friendly conditions. The weather says otherwise. I have this game at 8.5-9 runs, which gives us significant edge on the under.

the_data.label: Imanaga HR/9 (2026)
the_data.context: Worst in MLB, but wind helps

the_data.label: Ginn BB% (2026)
the_data.context: High, but walks don't score alone

all_picks.the_market: Total opened at 10.0-10.5 depending on book. FanDuel has 10.5 at -118 under, which is the best price. The market is pricing the pitchers' weaknesses but not the weather.
all_picks.weather_park: 82°F, 0% precip, 13 mph wind blowing in from left-center. This is a significant suppressor of fly ball distance at Wrigley.
all_picks.case_against: If the wind shifts (Wrigley is notorious for this), the game could turn into a slugfest. Both pitchers have blow-up potential, and one bad inning from either could push this over.
all_picks.what_were_betting_on: Wind staying in, both starters getting through 5 innings without a disaster, and the Cubs' defense turning batted balls into outs.
all_picks.scott_bot_quip: The market sees two HR-prone arms. The wind sees dinner.
all_picks.slate_assessment: Seven-game MLB slate plus Stanley Cup Final Game 2 tonight. Chris Sale dominates the board at home vs. Toronto, the Hurricanes need a bounce-back in a high-scoring series, and Wrigley's wind blowing in creates a pitcher-friendly environment for the Cubs-A's total.

At Wrigley, 13 mph wind blowing in suppresses the Cubs-A's total despite two HR-prone starters.
